Scatter is like Skeletor - Lord of Destruction and Overlord of Evil. kVp is the weapon of choice for contrast. It's the equivalent of He-Man's "power sword," in the fight against Skeletor (scatter). Image Optimization Tactics -Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech- Part 3: “Contrast Continued” “S/P Ratio” — On occasion you may hear people mention a thing called the "S/P Ratio. ” This is a ratio of the Secondary X-Rays (scatter) vs the Primary X- Rays (useful information) that strike the film / detector. So “improving your S/P Ratio” means reducing scatter. Low numbers are better (because the bad guy is the numerator). S/P Ratio essentially depends on two things: (1) The Thickness of the Body Part, and (2) The Area of the Beam. Thicker bodies and bigger areas are going to create more scatter - and increase the S/P ratio. Ways to Reduce Scatter (Improve Contrast): 1 - Collimate: This reduces the area of the beam. 2 - Compress the Part: This reduces the thickness (this is why mammograms are done in compression). 3 - Lower kVp: This reduces Compton scatter (increase P.E.) - trade off is in…

Image Optimization Tactics -Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech- Part 4: “Clinical Scenarios Continued” Scenario 2: Newborns When you x-ray an infant you are going to do a couple of things differently (which makes them testable). (1) You do NOT use a Grid. (2) You lower the kVp - “good technique is around 65 kVp.” They are small and don’t require a lot of juice to penetrate. *Most adult CXRs are around 120-140 kVp. (3) You use around the same or lower mAs - “good technique is around 2-4 mAs.” Adults are typically around 4 mAs (portables are usually closer to 2.5). ^ CHAPTER 4: OMA©l DETECT®®! - PROMETHEUS UONHART, M.D. - I Digital vs Analog Long ago, when dinosaurs roamed the earth, radiology was done on film screen. Now in the modem age it is digital. The comparison of film screen vs digital radiology could lend itself to multiple choice questions. “Larger Dynamic Range” One of the major limitations of film is its limited dynamic range. What that means is that there is a limit to the range of exposure intensities that it can accurately detect. In other words - at the extreme ends (not enough and too much) film screen sucks, leaving it very sensitive to under or overexposure. The consequence of that is more repeat studies (more radiation for the patient). A second consequence of that is for situations when you want to look at two structures that differ greatly in x-ray attenuation (example - bones and soft tissue) - film doesn’t optimize this as w…

Prom etheus, I don’t understand why film screen is not able to optimize seeing bone and soft tissue (structures that differ greatly in X-ray attenuation) ? By popular demand, I’m gonna make an attempt to explain this confusing topic. Before we discuss the limitation, we should quickly review this concept of “contrast.” When we talk about contrast we are referring to the differences in density (white vs black) that are present on a single image. For example, if you have big differences (example 1) you would say the image has “high radiographic contrast.” If you have a more narrow range of differences (example 2 ) you would say the image has “low radiographic contrast.” Example 2 : So why might one image have high contrast and another image have low contrast? Well, in the setting of an old fashion film screen it is going to depend on multiple factors including the choice of intensifying sc…

This is why the fixed narrow dynamic range issue is usually cited as the primary weakness in film imaging. If you look at the curve the bottom part (the toe) will result in an image with no significant contrast as the image is UNDER exposure. The top part of the curve (the shoulder) will also result in an image with no significant contrast as this image is OVER exposed. So - again this is the primary advantage of digital imaging over screen film. The wide dynamic range allow you to compensate for sub-optimal settings, and allows you to maximize contrast regardless of the technique that is used (within reason). 86

Dose Another element of “Digital vs Analog" that I want to point out is the superior dose efficiency - given: (1) The wider dynamic range, which would allow you to underexpose a little bit knowing that you can correct it by windowing, (2) You don’t have to depend on kVp so very much for contrast. With film kVp was super super important for getting your contrast (remember small differences in Z = big differences in photoelectric absorption). But with digital, since you can adjust contrast and brightness afterwards, you can go up on the kVp (by 15%) and half your mA to decrease dose. Frat Version = Digital has the potential to have lower dose relative to film because of post processing options. Gamesmanship = Another way to word this is “Higher Dose Efficiency.” Thats a fancy way to say less radiation. Spatial Resolution — “Pixels” Film radiographs were/are actually made up of tiny deposit…

Rapid Review - Differences in Plain Film vs Digital • Digital has “larger dynamic range” which means you can over or under expose the film (within reason) and it’s still diagnostic. This means less repeats (so less radiation overall). • Digital has a linear response curve, Film has a curvilinear response curve. • Film depends on kVp for contrast. Digital doesn’t as much - so you can use the 15% rule (increase your kVp by 15%, decrease your mA by 50%) and cut down on dose. • Spatial Resolution of Film may still be better than Digital Autom atic Exposure Control - (AEC) Honestly, I probably should have described this thing already - but I might need to mention it later in the chapter so I’ll go head and do it now. Bit Depth Pixels have what is called “bit depth, ” which is basically the number of bits that determine the number of shades of gray that can be displayed on a computer monitor. For example a 12-bit depth can display 4096 shades of gray, whereas a 14- and 16-bit depth can display 16,384 and 65,536 shades of gray, respectively. Where do those numbers come from ? It is basically 2 to the “x” power. So 2 12 = 4096. Take-home po…

- CR - Computed Radiography or “The C assette Based System" Storage Phosphors (CR) : These can work with a conventional x-ray machine (just like a normal cassette) The system uses a special phosphor (Barium Fluorohalides) that does not emit the absorbed energy as light after interaction with an x-ray. Instead, the x- ray causes an electron in the phosphor to change to a metastable state (one that it can hold for several days). Essentially when the plate is exposed to x-rays some of electrons within the phosphor are removed. Those liberated electrons get trapped in a “conduction band.” A magical metal called “europium” helps convince some of the liberated electrons to stay trapped in this conduction band for hours. The idea is that the storage phosphor is holding a “latent” x-ray image. When the cassette is returned back to a docking station a readout is performed. You can think of this l…

- DR - Digital Radiography or “The C assetteless System " Flat Panel Detectors (DR): When most people say “digital detector,” this is what they are talking about. It’s much faster than conventional fdm development or CR plate reading. As mentioned before you can have direct or indirect versions of digital systems. What does that mean again ? Indirect systems (like CR) have an intermediate step where the latent image has to be released as light and then converted into a digital signal (0 ’s and l ’s) to be displayed on the screen. • Indirect (scintillators) = X rays — ► Light — ► Charge • Direct (photoconductors) = X rays — ► Charge Indirect: The x-ray activates the Thallium-doped Cesium Iodide (Csl). Csl is the poster child scintillator. Scintillators Gonna Scintillate (emit light in response to an absorbed x-ray) Light gets emitted - which is then converted by a photodiode into an elect…

Lateral Dispersion of Light = Loss of Spatial Resolution A plague upon the indirect method is this idea of lateral light dispersion. What you hope would happen What actually happens Light goes straight down Lateral Dispersion of the Light What is this “Lateral Dispersion ” ? This is a problem you run into with indirect methods that use phosphors. Light tends to diffuse laterally after it leaves the site of conversion from an x-ray. This creates issues with spatial resolution, which get worse with increasing thickness of the crystal. However, making the crystal thin has its own problems as your sensitivity for collecting x-rays is going to drop off (they just fly right through). Is there a difference in “Lateral Dispersion ” between types o f phosphors? Yes - Gadolinium Oxysulfide has more lateral dispersion (light scatter) than Csl. How does one solve the problem o f “Lateral Dispersion …

Direct: As mentioned on the prior page, one of the issues with indirect methods is this extra step where x-rays are converted to light first, prior to being converted to an electric signal. This step is removed with direct methodology. • This uv That: Indirect uses a scintillator. Direct uses a photoconductor • This vs That: Indirect uses Cesium Iodine (a scintillator). Direct uses Amorphous Selenium, (the photoconductor) • This vs That: Both Indirect and Direct use a TFT read out array How it Works: Step 1: Apply a homogeneous “bias” charge to the surface of the selenium. Step 2: Fire the x-rays through the patient, into the selenium. The x-rays are absorbed by the selenium and electrons are released. Step 3: Released electrons ( “electron hole pair”) travel to the surface of the selenium and neutralize a portion of the applied charged. This is done in proportion to the radiation intens…

F ill Factor What is this “Fill Factor” ? This is the area of the detector which is sensitive to x-rays (in relation to the entire detector area). The higher the fill factor, the more efficient the detector. Fill Factor Differences: With DR systems, the electric field shaping essentially allows for a fill factor of nearly 100%. This is not seen with Indirect (CR) systems. Remember, differences between things make good multiple choice questions. CHAPTER S: M A M M ® - PROMETHEUS LIONHART, M.D. - Gamesmanship - When you are studying mammo for physics I want you to concentrate on what makes mammo unique - relative to regular plain old diagnostic x-ray. Remember by learning via comparison you are training yourself to exclude distractors - a key tactic for multiple choice exams. Overview — Why does mammo need to be different? The difference in attenuation of a breast cancer and just regular breast tissue is very small. So you have to use lower energy and a nearly mono-energetic beam to enhance the attenuation differences. Additionally, an increase in spatial resolution is required to see micro calcifications. fill® Low er Energy Why lower energy? - Remember the photoelectric effect dominates at lower kVp. Small differences really amplify the chance of the P.E. because of the cubed mathematic relationship (probability o f P.E. direct…

D ifferent T arg et Target Anodes: Mammo uses a molybdenum or rhodium anode (general radiology uses tungsten). Why? Remember, characteristic x-rays are “characteristic” because they depend on the binding energy of the shell involved, and this binding energy is different for different targets. So its unique... or “characteristic.” Tungsten , which is used in conventional imaging, has characteristic x-rays around 69.5 keV. Mammo needs energies way lower than that (ideally between 16 -23 keV). So the solution is to use a low atomic number target. The classic examples being molybdenum and rhodium. These targets generate lower K-characteristic x-rays which are required to generate all those false positives and unnecessary biopsies that mammo uses to fund its section. I mean... to find the cancers. The Normal Spectrum o f Molybdenum: The combination of low kVp and a low Z give Moly a high peak…

' J K-Edge Filtration K Edge Filtration - A K. edge filter is placed on the outside of the tube with the goal of creating a nearly mono-energetic beam in the target range of 16-23 keV. Moly is classically used for this, taking advantage of the 20 keV K edge and subsequent rise in photoelectric absorption. Energies lower than 15 and greater the 20 get filtered out. : Spectrum of Molybdenum with Superimposed Mo Filter 18 keV 18 keV Rhodium: An alternative to Moly is Rhodium, which has a similar (slightly stronger) spectrum, and a binding energy of 20.2 keV. High Yield: • Filters block BOTH high and low photons, with the benefit of removing the low energy photons which only give you dose and the high energy photons which won’t help with contrast. • Rh/Rh is used for larger or denser breasts - because it’s higher energy compared to Mo/Mo. • Mo anode with Rhodium filter is also used to produc…

Focal Spot I mA I Exposure Tim e Focal Spot Size: As mentioned before, mammo requires a better spatial resolution than you get with normal imaging. To achieve this you use a smaller focal spot: High Yield: • Mammography uses a focal spot of 0.1 and 0.3 mm • General X-ray uses focal spots of 0.6 and 1.2 mm A smaller focal spot is not going to tolerate heat as well. To address this you have to use a lower mA (otherwise you’d melt the anode). The mA is limited to 50 for 0.1 mm, and 100 for 0.3 mm. Another issue is that since you are using a lower mA you need a longer exposure time. Generally you still try and keep the exposure time under 2 seconds. -Prometheus, Why do we have to angle the tube 20 deg? What is that doing to the energy on the anode side? It does nothing to the anode angle. The anode angle is not the same as the tube tilt angle. The tube is angled / oriented for a couple of reasons but it is easiest to just think about it as an attempt to maximize the effects of the heel effect by giving more x-rays to the chest wall (denser / thicker part of the tissue). i S L ** diagrams show a change in the tube tilt angle. Notice the anode angle doesn’t change (because it is built into the machine). Now - a possible trick could be to calculate the effective target angle. What this means is the anode angle plus the tube tilt. So for example, you would have the same field coverage (effective target angle) if you had: Anode Angle 8 + Tube Tilt 16 = 24 Or Anode Angle 16 + Tube Tilt 8 = 24 102

T he B eryllium W indow: Most diagnostic tubes use pyrex glass - this is NOT used in mammo because it causes excessive attenuation of the energies used in mammo. Instead a beryllium exit window is used. FRAT Version: • Regular Dx uses Glass, • Mammo uses Beryllium . Window Com pression Mammography is performed in compression (another unique feature). Why do they do that? There are lots of advantages. Mainly it makes for a more uniform tissue - gives you less overlap, less geometric blurring, and better detail. You would have a TON of call backs if you didn’t do it... even more than you do already .The most testable advantage is probably the dose / scatter reduction. Here is the full list of reasons: • Reduced Thickness = Less Scatter = Lower kVp can be chosen • Lower kVp and Less Scatter = Improved Contrast • Reduced Thickness = Less mAs needed = Less Dose • Breast Doesn’t Move = Less Mo…

Grid Anti-Scatter Grid: Since the breast is placed in compression and you are using lower kVp (both of which intrinsically reduce scatter) a smaller grid ratio is used. Mammo uses a 4-5 grid ratio (general x-ray uses a 6-16). What is a “Grid Ratio” ? Dose with the Grid - The grid removes scattered photons - which reduces some dose. However, the same technique can’t be used with a grid or you will underexpose. So, the p atj0 _ (_j/yy technique is turned up. Ultimately the dose is INCREASED with a grid. H= height, W= width between grids Bucky Factor is 2 for Mammo and 5 for General X-Ray M agnification - P art 1 Mammo is also unique in its implementation of the “mag view” - traditionally used for calcs. How it s done: The breast is moved away from the detector , and closer to the source (focal spot). Typically the distance to the source is cut in half -which doubles the mag. Focal Spot Mag…

M agnification V ie w - P art 2 “T h e A ir G ap” “Dai Air Gap ” - I call it. So remember when we bring the boob further away from the target (and closer to the source) we are creating magnification. In doing this we are also creating a “gap” of air between the boob and target. This gap essentially acts like an anti scatter grid. Remember this ofif-axis scatter is coming out of the patient (in this case the patient’s boob). The gap allows it to miss the target. Key Point - No Grid for Mag Views, Instead You have an Air Gap. m m “Dat Air Gap” Off Axis Scatter - Hopefully missing the target. D igital M am m ography Digital systems have the major advantage of allowing you to change the contrast and brightness after the acquisition of the image by adjusting the window and level. Pixel Size and Spatial Resolution: Remember that smaller pixels = better spatial resolution. Additionally, you can lose spatial resolution from spread of electronic or light signal inside the digital or computed detectors. In general, most digital systems have lower spatial resolution (around 5-11 lp/mm) - relative to analog (1 1 -1 3 lp/mm). • Another point of trivia is that the MQSA does NOT have specific line pair requirements for digital; instead they are linked to the manufacturer’s specs. Dose: Digital machines have 15% less dose compared to analog (1.8mGy for analog, 1.6 for digital). This is because the beam quality is better. Digital also has fewer repeat exams (because you can window and leve…

I ’ve made an effort to include a lot of repetition on topics (especially confusing topics) in this chapter. Just like with mammo, the differences between regular diagnostic X-Ray, and fluoro lend themselves easily to multiple choice questions. So what is different? Regular diagnostic x-ray is performed with mA ranging between 200-800 and very short exposure times. For fluoro to work it has to have very long exposure times. The trade-off is, that if you want long exposure times and you don’t want to melt your focal spot (or the patient), you will need to decrease the mA. Speaking of the focal spot - to limit geometric blurring, a smaller focal spot is used. Regular Dx Fluoro mA 200-800 mA 0-5 kVp 50-120 kVp 50-12…

Im a g e In te n s ifie r Smaller than input - so it Concentrates them Electrons -> Light “Minification Gain” Accelerating Electrons, Between Voltage Differences: “Flux Gain” Output Phosphor Focusing Electrodes Light -> Electrons Captures X-Rays, Turns them to Light Photo- Cathode Input Phosphor: Csl v> <> X-Rays Grid The purpose of an image intensifier is to convert x-rays to light and then back to electrons. Along the course of this conversion, the electron flux and energy are amplified by the image intensifier with the end result being a greater amount of light emerging from the output phosphor. To actually view the pictures, you must couple an image intensifier through lenses to a TV camera. Noticing the word ‘‘Gain ” is showing up on that diagram? E lectro static Focusing Lens: The “electron lens ” is a series of electrodes that are used to focus the electron beam as it flows from the photocathode towards the output phosphor. Starting at the Input Phosphor: • X-Rays have recently been converted to Light. • Light to be converted into electrons via the Photo Cathode. • Notice a slight curve in the Input Phosphor? This is to maintain the same travel distance to the central region (necessary to undistorted focusing) Input Phosphor Focusing Electrodes: • A series of electrodes work to focus the beam and speed up the flow (“flux gain”). Output Phosphor: • Output Phosphor works to convert electrons into a pattern of light. • Note that we are not dealing with X-Rays. Relative to a conventional phosphor this can be much thinner (because it is electrons to x-rays). • The benefit of keeping this thin is improved (or maintained) resolution in…

Gain “Brightness Gain” - This is an old term describing the ability of the I.I to convert x-ray energy into light energy and then increase the brightness. Think about this as the increase in emitted light from the I.I. compared to the amount of light you would have obtained if the I.I. was never invented (and you just directly exposed the phosphorescent screen). Testable Point: Brightness Gain is the combined effects of Flux Gain and Minification Gain. Brightness Gain = Flux Gain x Minification Gain “Flux Gain” This describes the increase in magnitude of light coming from the output phosphor, relative to the input. This increase in magnitude is accomplished with a high voltage between the photocathode and the output phosphor (25-35 kV). The voltage causes electrons to accelerate and yields a gain in energy. Frat Version: Accelerating Electrons Increases Brightness “Minification Gain” Ele…

Geometric vs Electronic Magnification Not all Mag is created equal. There are two primary types - and obviously the difference is testable. Geometric Magnification - This is the type of magnification that I discussed previously in the the book (most recently in the mammo chapter). As a quick refresher: to magnify something, you generally bring it closer to the x-ray source Source to Object Distance MORE Magnification Magnification Object to Detector Distance People usually think about the “inverse square law” for reducing radiation, but obviously it works the other way too. If you get closer to the tube you get more radiation— and it doesn’t double, it squares. Electronic Magnification (Zoom) - If you decrease the input field of view by half, then only one fourth of the input phosphor will be irradiated. If all other parameters are held constant, the brightness will also drop to one quar…

More Meat - S patial Resolution vs M agnification - This can be confusing, so let me spend a little extra time on it. I’ll start by talking about electronic or zoom magnification. The image intensifier can, via various forms of magic and spell casting, vary the size of the input radiation field, but still keeping the output field the same size. If for example the input field is cut in half then the size of the object being viewed is also cut in half but the output is still fixed. So relative to the original setting, the object has undergone a 2x magnification - and a double in spatial resolution. **Remember the dose goes up because halving the input field o f view, while keeping all the other factors the same, would cut the image brightness to a 1/4 o f the original brightness. To compensate for this, the amount o f radiation at the input o f the image intensifier must be quadrupled to c…

- Now let us pivot to the confusing part. Geometric Mag - This is the kind of magnification you get by fucking around with the object-film distance and focus to film distance. The general rule (under standard conditions) is to avoid this by following two rules : (1) Keep the object as close to the film as possible (2) Keep the focus-film distance as large as possible Why is it so bad? Well, distortion and unsharpness right ? Things that lower spatial resolution that go hand in hand with this type of magnification. So why am I acting like there is confusion ? Cuz.... Mammo. Fucking Mammo Dude. Here is one more reason to hate mammo. Mag Views - they improve spatial resolution so you can see those low grade DCIS calcs better. Remember all that air gap crap? NO grid etc.... ? Ok - so let me try and explain. Magnification is produced by moving the object away from the film and closer to the f…

Another Way to Conceptualize This: If the objects are moved farther away from the detector, the divergence of the beam will increase and allow the objects to be projected onto different detector elements. Therefore resulting in greater ability to resolve them as two (instead of one) which is the definition of spatial resolution. T This leads me into my final point on this topic. The idea of ‘''Receptor Unsharpness. ’ Technically are three contributors to “unsharpness.’ (1) Geometric - the stuff we have been discussing (2) Patient - Patients are not the ideal mathematical target. This is because you have a thickness that is going to vary - typically decreasing x-ray attenuation towards the edges. The gradual change in attenuation at the edges can (and does) produce an indistinct edge. You also have organs moving inside the body which create blur and contribute to unsharp edges. You get th…

OK ...so where am I ’m going with this ? You can think of receptor unsharpness as the ultimate limit on resolution. Resolution in a digital system is limited by the size of the pixels of the captured image. The relationship between how good the receptor “intrinsic Receptor resolution” is and the Focal spot size (R/F) will ultimately determine if geometric magnification will increase or decrease the spatial resolution. If R/F is greater than about 0.5, then magnification radiography will reduce unsharpness, a value less than 0.5 and magnification will increase unsharpness. For the purpose of multiple choice, when dealing with general radiography R/F « 0.5 (intrinsic receptor resolution ~0.1 mm, focal spot size ~1 mm) so resolution decreases (blurring increases) with increasing magnification. In mammography with 0.3 mm (contact) and 0.1 mm (magnification) that focal spot size -is gonna be …

Dose & C om pensation For G eom etric Mag: So, if geometric mag is so bad - what do you do about it? You can try and collimate. Collimation is electronically controlled with positioning changes made to irradiate a small region of anatomy - you change to a smaller input surface. The trick is that you are actually minifying the image less, as a smaller input surface is projected on the same output surface. Best Position o f the I.I. and X-ray Tube? X- ray tube far away, with the I.I. close. Having the image intensifier as close to the patient as possible does 3 things: (1) Decreases patient dose. •The ABC tries to make pretty pictures and more incident x-rays are detected when the 1.1. is close so the ABC doesn’t increase mA (to increase the number of x-rays generated) to make an adequate image. (2) Decreases scatter to the operator. •Less x-rays are made to scatter and less scatter escape…

A S m aller Field of V ie w Does W hat to Dose? So, the important (testable) distinction here is smaller FOV from collimation alone vs smaller FOV from magnification. Sm aller FOV - Collim ation Collimation is a type of beam restriction. In the most general sense, a restrictor is a device that “restricts” the x-ray beam at the opening of the x-ray tube. They come in three main flavors with collimators being the most testable. Collimators do two things well and have one negative side effect. • Pro: They Reduce Dose • Pro: They Reduce Scatter • Con: They Alter Exposure • Dose Reduction: The smaller the field of view, the less volume of the patient is irradiated. Remember that even a little bit of collimation goes along way because area is a square function. • Scatter Reduction: The smaller the field of view, the less scatter radiation (per unit size). The opposite is true also - bigger FOV…

Sm aller FOV - M agnification As we discussed on the prior pages, the image intensifier can vary the size of the input radiation field, but still keeps the output field the same size. If the input field is cut in half then the size of the object being viewed is also cut in half but the output is still fixed. Remember the dose goes up because halving the input field of view, while keeping all the other factors the same, would cut the image brightness to a 1/4 of the original brightness. To compensate for this, the amount of radiation at the input of the image intensifier must be quadrupled to compensate for reduction in juice. More radiation = More Dose. Let’s use this as an opportunity to discuss the topic of “Minification Gain ” - a made up word used by PhDs to sound smart, in more depth. The idea of “Minification Gain” can be thought of like the magnifying glass focusing the awesome po…

OK... but now let us work this problem backwards. Remember that this system (the 1.1.) magnifies shit by changing the input (not the output). Although, the actual way this change in field size ( “change from 9 inch mode to 6 inch mode”) is accomplished is by changing the focal zone by applying voltage. The higher the voltage, the more the beam is focused. Change in Focal Zone (by spell casting) Unchanged Output Size Less Total Electrons Used, Since only seeing the central part of the beam Utilized Electrons Utilized Electrons The ratio of out to in, makes the one using just the central part of the beam less “minified.” This decrease in minification gives you the appearance of the TV screen that you have magnified something. The problem is that you have used less total electrons, and as you have experienced this “minification loss.” In vs Out Utilized Electrons Comparing in to Out... The …

Ok putting this together now. Smaller FOV “Mode” (achieved by using voltage to move the focal point, so that central core of electrons are utilized). Now... to nearly complete this journey lets discuss the idea of “Brightness Gain.” Brightness Gain is the product of two unrelated things. 1- Minification Gain = Same energy in concentrated on a smaller target (like the magnifying glass) increases the density of the energy (Brightness). 2- Flux Gain = A gain in the ability of the electrons in the I.I. system to produce light photons. You may think, 1 electron in = 1 photon of light on the way out. BUT because of the acceleration process (1 electron can = 50 light photons). So this amplifies the process (or reduces it if you are using less electrons). Text Book Answer: Brightness Gain = Minification Gain x Flux Gain Practical Answer to Reason Through MCQs = Minification Gain = Brightness Gai…

But Prometheus! How Does that Cause More Dose ? Automatic Exposure Rate Control (AERC) circuit (the artist formerly known as Automatic Brightness Control - ABC). This guy has one job... keep the signal to noise ratio happy. As long as signal to noise ratio is happy everything will be fine. It does this by monkeying around with the X-Ray exposure rate on the input phosphor of the I.I. (or flat panel detector). Per my life changing discussion above, the brightness gain of the I.I. decreases as the magnification increases. This fucks up the SNR (less signal), and the AERC circuitry spazes out by boosting the exposure rate. How much does it increase the rate ? For the purpose of multiple choice, I would say equal to the ratio of FOV areas. Example = It’s a typical day on Fluoro. You are performing a post op swallow study on a 600 pound, unemployed, Hep C positive, chain smoker who has eaten …

So - let us just back up a second and be clear. If you traveled back in time to the 1970s, and you went to the 1.1. store you’d find that they come in a bunch of different sizes (23, 30, 35, 40 cm). That is the standard FOV diameter. The smaller ones (23s) are used for cardiac imaging... since cardiac imaging has been totally hijacked by our good friends the Cardiologists. Ok., back on track. Into the Weeds with Electronic Mag and Dose To touch on the next bit of fluoro related dose trivia, I need to briefly introduce the idea of KERMA and Kerma Area Product (KAP). I’ll mention it again later in the text. KERMA = Kinetic Energy Release per unit MAss. The best way to understand KERMA is to think of it as the TOTAL amount of energy deposited from the ionizing radiation. With that TOTAL amount divided by a unit of mass to give it a more quantifiable dose. Why did I capitalize TOTAL? Because when the radiation strikes a unit mass it will not only create some movement of particles (kinetic energy), but also some amount of heat. Just measuring what goes on with the electrons is not the TOTAL amount of energy (there is also that heat). As a result, some smart people figured out that the best way to measure both of these components was to measure it at the moment just before energy w…

Position the patient away from the source, and close to the I.I. / Detector 2. Use the smallest field of view by collimating (this also improves contrast) 3. Avoid magnification

Along those lines, the Uncle Sam has set regulations to limit Air Kerma rates - to minimize peak skin doses. That regulation is 87 mGy per min. WTF would they pick 87 and not 90 or 100 ? Artifacts Pincushion Distortion: With a large field of view you can sometimes get the appearance of bent lines at the periphery (lines that should be straight). The inward bowing pattern is said to resemble a pincushion. S Distortion: This is a similar artifact to the pincushion, also seen more in larger fields of view. The etiology of this artifact is an interference of the earth’s magnetic field on the flow of electrons heading towards the I.I. Making it better? The addition of a mythical material called “mu metal” supposedly can deflect the magnetic field (and protect against vampires). Pincushion Distortion - Due to Large FOV S-Distortion - Due to Earth’s magnetic field Flare or Glare Artifact: If you have a transition from heavy attenuation to minimal attenuation - you can see bright white “Glare” at the periphery near the decreased attenuation. This is from an overproduction of x-r…

Vignetting Artifact: Because distances from the focusing point to the outer phosphor tend to vary, with the closest path in the center and the farthest path at the edge, you can end up with a dark periphery and a light center. Vignetting Artifact - Edges are darker than center Saturation Artifact: If the dose is cranked up to try and penetrate a very dense object (classically metal), you can end up with regions around the metal appearing very bright. Saturation Artifact The I.I. System has many limitations - one of which is dynamic range. The way to think about this is that the receptor requires a goldilocks amount of radiation - too much and it saturates (turns totally white) too little and it under penetrates (too dark). You will see this saturation - which they can also call "flare" or "veiling" - in two scenarios 1- the incident X-ray beam passes through low density anatomy (like the…

Fluoroscopic M echanism Subtypes There are two different types: (1) The dinosaur one “Image Intensifiers ”, and (2) The new one “Flat Panel Detector. ” I ’ve completed my discussion of the dinosaur one (the 1.1.). Now let us turn our attention to the modern type - the Flat Panel Detector SVs t e t f ' G rid V Rays V Entrance Surface of FPD “Carbon Fiber - to protect components Csl Needles Act as a Phosphor Turns Photons into Light Photodiode Array Absorbs light and converts it into electrons Read Out Elem ent ____ (T ransistors and G ates) Determine charge of elements in photodiode array and construct image. No television camera is needed in FPD systems As you can see all that I.I. stuff doesn’t happen with the modem system (oh, don’t worry you will still get tested on it). 135

Vocab: “Pitch ” There is a thing called pitch. Essentially it is the linear dimension of a detector element. This is different than the “pitch” you think about with CT (more on that later). “Fill Factor” There is a thing called fill factor. The system isn’t 100% efficient, only a portion is actually sensitive to light. The ratio of the sensitive area over the total area is the fill factor. As the detector element gets smaller, you get better spatial resolution, but the “fill factor” decreases. In other words, a smaller detector element will have superior resolution but will require more radiation. “M atrix” - The matrix is the number of detector elements (or pixels) on the surface of the FPD - in each dimension (horizontal and vertical). 1 Detector Element = 1 Pixel Math problem: If the matrix size is 1100 x 1100, and the FOV is 25cm, what is the pixel size (or detector element)? the for…

Binning: There is a thing called binning. Taking several detector elements (DELs) and making a large DEL. The idea is the you reduce the amount of data (and reduce Quantum Mottle - less variation in x-ray photons from pixel to pixel). o o o o o o o o o o o o oooo 4 Pixels x 4 Pixels 2 Pixels x 2 Pixels 1 Pixels x 1 Pixels *No Binning * 2 x 2 Binning * 4 x 4 Binning Less Mottle means you can reduce radiation and keep the same noise. Larger DEL does reduce the spatial resolution When is “Binning” especially useful? -W ith large FOV (when there are too many pixels in the image) If there is no “binning, ” the spatial resolution changes in what way for different fields o f view (in a FPD system)? No change. If you have binning, large FOVs will have less spatial resolution (but can use less radiation to maintain the noise level). Remember “binning” is a FPD thing, not an I.I. thing What usuall…

A rtifacts (FPD): Are artifacts seen with I.I.s the same as FPDs? Nope. FPDs do NOT have pincushion, S distortions, vignette, glare, or saturation artifacts Bad Pixel - appear as either white or black spots. One method to correct for this (often built into the system) is to interpolate in order to fill the data. Lag Artifact (Ghosting) can also occur with FPD. This tends to occur if the exposure uses very high radiation. S patial Resolution: What limits spatial resolution o f an I.I. fluoro system? -The television display system. The resolution of a TV depends on the raster (scan) lines, the bandwidth, and the FOV. Does the display limit the spatial resolution o f a FPD system? M onkeying Around w ith th e kVp Depending on the study you are doing, you may need to monkey around with the kVp to optimize the exam. Barium vs Iodine: In the next few pages I’m going to talk about fluoro in Angio, but I might as well introduce this idea now. Remember this idea of a “k-edge” ? It’s the energy where absorption (and therefore image contrast) is maximized. Usually for a study using IV contrast (iodine) you are going to pick a kV of around 70 - which will place the average energy around 35 - landing it flush on the k-edge of Iodine (which is 33 keV). But what about Barium? Most of fluoro is barium, and the k-edge of barium is pretty similar to iodine (it’s like 37). So it s gonna be the same right? Fluoro in IR Focal Spot: You are trying to visualize small things (tiny vessels in real time) - this means you need a small focal spot + large number of x-rays. To deal with this small focal spot, local heat exchanges must be added to avoid melting the whole unit. The anode angle is smaller when compared to conventional x-ray. The smaller angle leads to an increased heel effect. Fortunately, in IR the heel effect remains zero because you use a small field of view and small image detector (only allows the central portion of the x-ray beam to be imaged). Focal spots can be exchanged (large, small, micro) - depending on the need for resolution. kVp: As mentioned on the prior page, the best kVp to use with contrast is between 60-80 kVp (average beams hit that k-edge nicely). A higher kVp loses iodine contrast. Filter: There is a thing called an “equalization filter” or a “soft filter.” These…

Patient Dose Most IR systems use a pulsed fluoro (helps reduce dose). 50% of the dose is delivered in the superficial 3-5 cm of skin/fat • The depth of this 50% depends on the kVp and filtration (higher kVp + Copper Filtration = more penetration) • For a body (or body part) measuring less than 10 cm (a baby for example) - the grid should be off • A thicker (fatter) patient gets more skin dose. This is because automatic brightness control sees less penetration - then cranks up the dose. • Additional high dose situations: lateral and oblique views Patient and operator dose doubles with a lateral view (compared to PA) • The typical dose is about 0.3 - 0.5 mGy per frame at the entrance skin position (lOx - 20x more per image than fluoro) Total Dose = (dose per frame) x (frame rate) x (duration x number of runs) “Source to Skin Distance” (SSD) = how close the patient is relative to the x-ray …

Dose Area Product (Kerma Area Product) This measures the radiation dose to air in mGy - multiplied by the collimator area - then reported in mGy/cm. The measurement is independent of beam location. It’s true that as you move the beam away from the patient the intensity decreases- BUT, the beam spreads out more. These two things occur in equal amounts so the DAP (KAP) is NOT dependent on location. DAP (KAP): Low Dose to Large Skin Area = High Dose to Small Skin Area Magnification - will increase Air Kerma, but NOT KAP Obviously this doesn’t help you grade risk of a skin bum. Instead it is used to estimate total energy deposited in patient, effective dose, and cancer risk. If something reduces the DAP (KAP) it probably also reduces the scatter and the patient’s dose. An option for high dose is “specially activated fluoro” or “high dose control”, where you can have rates up to 20 R/min. The trick is an audible alarm must be on when this is used. • Below 2 Gy - No action needed • 2-5 Gy - advise patient to watch for bums - especially 10 days post procedure • Above 5 Gy - procedure and dose should be reviewed by physics • 2 Gy - Early Transient Erythema • 3 Gy - Temporary Epilation (hair loss) • 6 Gy - Chronic Erythema / “Main Erythema” • 7 Gy - Permanent Epilation (hair loss) • 10 Gy - Telangiectasia • 13 Gy - Dry Desquamation • 18 Gy - Moist Desquamation / Ulceration • 24 Gy - Secondary ulceratio…

e CHAPTER 7: ■ PROMETHEUS UONHART, M.D. - Just like with mammo (and fluoro), the differences between regular diagnostic x-ray, and CT lend themselves easily to multiple choice questions. So what is different? Most CT scanners today are “3rd generation” - which means the x-ray tube and the detectors spin around the patient in synchrony. CT X-ray tubes use tungsten alloy targets placed on high speed rotating anodes. CT tubes are designed to operate at reasonable voltages (between 80 -140 kV) with very high tube currents up to 1000mA. CT scanners have both small (0.6mm) and large (1.2mm) focal spots. Typically you need a large focal spot to handle all that power - lOOkW. Vocabulary: What is this “Ray ” — A “ray” is a measure of total x-ray attenuation along a line from the focal point to a single detector Projection What is this “Sinogram ” - A “sinogram” is a bunch of squiggly lines that represent the data from all the projections of all the tube angles (0-360). What is this “Projection ” - A “projection” is all rays at a given angle of the x-ray tube. In other words, it’s a series of rays that pass through the patient - at the same orientation. Pitch Pitch is a super important (testable) concept. The big take-home is that you have a choice; dose or spatial resolution. If you overlap your scanning beams, you will have better spatial resolution but more dose. If you uncoil the beams, your dose becomes less - but your spatial resolution suffers. The concept is pretty easy and intuitive - but you need to know the vocab words to get the questions right. So lets learn them. Pitch: Pitch is defined as the distance the table moves during the time of a single revolution of the tube divided by the beam width. If the movement of the table is less than the beam width, you end up with a fraction (a number less than 1). If the Pitch = movement of the table is more than the beam width you end up with a whole number (a number more than 1). If they are equal, then obviously you get a 1. Table Movement Beam Width “Pitch of 1” - This mean there i…

The Hounsfield Unit: CT scanners generate a picture based on attenuation data (how many x-rays are blocked), but the important thing to understand is that the data acquired is all relative. The standard density of water is used as a zero point. Anything less dense than water is a negative number, and anything more dense than water is a positive number. The formula used to calculate HU is: HU = 1000 x (attenuation of material - attenuation of water) / attenuation of water. What is the relationship between HU and X-ray attenuation? When HU increases by 10 HU, x-ray attenuation increases by 1% Does changing the keVmess with the HU? Yup Why? I thought values were zero’d to water, and standard etc... ? Number are based on attenuation (blockage / absorption) of x-rays. Remember that changes in photoelectric interaction are going to change x-ray absorption. In general - since RE. dominates at l…

W indow W idth and Level: It’s a basic concept that is highly testable. The “Level” is the midpoint of the gray scale display (the “center”). You want your level at the attenuation of the thing you are interested in. For example, if you are interested in bone - you want a high level. The “window width” (aka “window”) is selected based on what you are comparing. If you are comparing things with very different densities you want a wide window. If you are comparing things with similar densities (example white and gray matter), you want a narrow window. How this concept can be tested? Really there are two main ways: (1) you can ask a typical window for lung, bone, liver, etc... This is partly memorization (see chart) but you can sorta figure it out based on the principals I mentioned above. (2) They can ask you below or above what level will give you a white or black reading. This kind of qu…

You have detectors rotating around the patient which are collecting the raw attenuation data (lots of 1 ’s and 0’s). This data is then processed in an algorithm (either filter back or the new iterative reconstruction). After that a filter or “kernel” is applied - either smoothing or sharpening the data. Acquiring th e R aw Data There are basically two methods for doing this: Axial Scanning and Helical Scanning. Axial The table is stationary. The tube comes “on” and takes a picture. The tube shuts off and the table moves up a slice. The tube comes back “on” and takes another picture. Advantages: Better spatial resolution in the z-dimension since full image sets are taken. No partial volume effect along the long axis. • Less Partial Volume Artifact. The artifacts of partial volume with helical CT are noticed more along a curved surface. Classic example = skull. The table moves at a constant speed. The tube is on the entire time. Advantages: • Primary Advantage = Way faster • Secondary Advantage = Post acquisition flexibility in the selection of slice location and lower probability of anatomic discontinuities between adjacent slices containing moving anatomy in the chest/abdomen scans. R aw D ate Projection Generating an image from the acquired data involves figuring out the linear attenuation coefficients of each pixel in the image matrix. In other words... math. Lots of math. Fortunately computers are good at math. There are a few different techniques for creating the image. Back Projection - This was the original way. After doing lots of math (literally millions of data points) - using sonograms etc... Eventually a value of attenuation is calculated for each pixel along a ray - traced through the patient. Filter Back Projection - This is the more modem way. Essentially a mathematical filter is applied prior to calculating the back projection. Just know it’s better than the old way - and it’s utilized by a lot of modem scanners now. Iterative Reconstruction - “Forwarded” information is compared to actual information and differences are used to correct the image. What…

Signal to Noise Remember that noise is “Data that contributes nothing useful to the image.” People often make the mistake of thinking that Quantum Mottle = Noise. It’s true that Quantum Mottle (essentially a lack of photons) is the major contributor to noise - but it is not the only contributor. Scatter is also going to contribute “nothing useful to the image,” and therefore is a factor. Why do I bring this up? Because it will make understanding how increasing the number of x-rays does not lead to a 1:1 improvement in signal to noise. It’s also a Poisson distribution thing - but I think that is harder to conceptualize. Signal to Noise is a better way to think about “noise”. Remember this is a quantitative method for comparing the signal (stuff that contributes to the image) vs the noise (stuff that doesn’t contribute to the image). If your signal is really strong - you can tolerate more …

S pecial Topic H alf V alue Layer (HVL) - ■ ■ /■ You may recall the term “Half Value Layer” from earlier in the text. As a refresher HVL refers to the thickness of a material which can attenuate an x-ray beam to 50% of its original intensity. Let’s review some trivia related to this: • In conventional radiography, the HVL of soft tissue in a human is assumed to be 3 cm. • In CT, the HVL of soft tissue in a human is also assumed to be 3-4 cm • In mammography, the HVL of soft tissue in a human is assumed to be 1 cm (because of the lower energy beam quality). C ontrast Resolution Contrast resolution can be defined as the ability to discriminate between structures with very similar attenuation characteristics (CT numbers). Spatial Resolution Spatial Resolution (frequency) -This is the ability to distinguish small objects that are close together (tell that they are separate). Practically, a bar pattern is used to measure this (line pairs per mm). Line pair resolution for CT is typically 0.5 to 1 Lp/mm - which is shit compared to mammo (remember that was like 11 Lp/mm). Factors that affect spatial resolution: Focal Spot Size • A larger focal spot, means the object details are spread out over several detectors. This degrades and blurs the image • Smaller focal spot = better spatial resolution Magnification More magnification blurs the image Detector Aperture Size • As the detector size is reduced the cranial-caudal resolution (z-axis) increases • The in-plane “x-y axis” is NOT affected by aperture size. Number o f Projections • More projections, more data. Better Resolution Reconstruction Slice Thickness The …

• As pitch increases, so does the width of the slice sensitivity profile (SSP). • As the SSP widens, the slice thickness increases. Thicker slices = Less Spatial Resolution. Spatial Resolution as a function o f Pitch: Quick Review o f Pitch • Doses in helical scanning with a pitch of 1.0 are similar to those from axial scanning. • If the pitch is < 1.0 then the dose increases because the slices overlap. • If the pitch is > 1.0 then the dose decreases because energy is more spread out. • The relationship is proportional. A pitch of 2 halves the dose. A pitch of 0.5 doubles the dose. Patient Motion (why it is important to ask the patient to hold his/her breath... and also to stop masturbating - at least during the scan) Voluntary and involuntary movement leads to blurring. • Blurring = reduced image resolution Spatial Resolution Related to Filters: Sharp Filter = Better Spatial Resolution …

This increases spatial resolution but decreases contrast resolution (less photons per box) • Holding matrix size constant and increasing FOV will increase pixel size. This decreases spatial resolution but increases contrast resolution (more photons per box). Protocol Modification: Big Fat Ass Quantum Mottle - The Fat Man’s Burden. As you may recall, Quantum Mottle is the result of an insufficient quantity of photons striking the detector. The fatter you are, the more photons you absorb, and the less signal reaches the detector. The consequence is a shitty signal / noise ratio. Noisy images are a plague on the super monstrously gigantic fat (very fat) extra fat-fat patient. Solutions: • Eat Less and Exercise: Impractical due to the delicious taste of cheeseburgers and McDonald’s french fries (“freedom fries” I call them). Plus... cardio sucks. • mA: The simple solution is to increase the mA. As we discussed with all that fancy math you need to quadruple the mA to double your SNR - or cut your relative noise level in half. The tradeoff is that doubling the mA will also double the dose. • mAs: If you increase the time per rotation of the gantry…

kVp Modifications - IV Contrast First, let us review the implication of changing photon energy (keV) on HUs. Yes, HUs are set to a “zero” by using a water phantom. However, the HU values always depend on the kV and to some degree filtration. This reliance on kV is all about the shit we talked about in the first few chapters, mainly atomic number and the “k-edge.” Refreshing Memories: An abrupt increase in the attenuation of photons occurs at a photon energy slightly more than the binding energy of the K shell electron of the particular atoms interacting with the photons. This is the sweet spot for photoelectric absorption of the photons - or the “k-edge”. Exploiting this is an important part of imaging (and more importantly - something that can be testable). Low Energy - Easily Attenuated K Edge - Spike Corresponds with Binding Energy Higher Energy - Pass Right Photon Energy This is take…

The important concept is that the amount of over beaming decreases the more detector rows you have. For example - we have two rows here. The top one shows only a few slices acquired simultaneously. The bottom one shows a larger amount acquired simultaneously. Hopefully you can see that the larger the beam width (more slices acquired simultaneously), the less the excess in dose. CT A rtifacts: Beam Hardening- As the x-ray beam passes through an object, the lower energy photons are removed preferentially, leaving a “harder beam” with an increased average energy. There are two artifacts associated with this. Cupping - The x-rays passing through the middle of a uniform shape (like a head) are hardened more than those traveling through the periphery (a shorter path). The harder the beam the slower the rate of attenuation. The manifestation is the center of the image appears darker than the peripheral portions. Normal Cupping Normal Dark Bands / Streak - This occurs in the setting of two dense objects. X-rays that pass through one are less attenuated than those that pass through both. The result is dark bands and streaks between those two objects. The classic location is bone or where a dense contrast was used. Fixing Beam Hardening: (1) Filtration - Pre hardening of…

Partial Volume: This can occur in two main ways. Pattern 1 ( “Partial Volume Effect”): A dense object protrudes partially into the width of an x-ray beam. This results in divergence of the beam and manifests as shading artifacts adjacent to said object. Pattern 2: CT voxels are 3D cubes. If you have a dense thing taking up half the cube, and a sparse (low attenuating) thing in the other half of the cube the machine will average the two together giving you something that has intermediate density. The classic location is the skull base averaging with CSF or brain to sorta look like blood. 1/2 Light Density End Product will be an 112 Dark Density average of the two Both Overlapping in a Single Voxel Fixing partial volume: Make your slices thinner. If the noise is a problem, acquire thin slices then generate thicker slices by adding them together. Photon Starvation: High attenuating areas (c…

Undersampling: An insufficient number of projections used to reconstruct the CT can diminish quality, and result in misregistration artifacts. There are two main types. View Aliasing: This is when you have under sampling between projections. You see fine stripes radiating from the edge of (but at a distance from) a dense object. This is fixed by acquiring the largest possible number of projections per rotation - slowing the rotation speed. Ray Aliasing: This is when you have under sampling within a projection. You see strips appearing close to the structure. This is fixed by using specialized high resolution techniques - manufacturer employed. Metal Artifact: Metal causes streak artifact. It does this through several mechanisms: beam hardening, partial volume, aliasing, and having density ranges higher than what can be handled by the computer. Testable trivia is that metals with high Z (…

Ring Artifact: A calibration error or defective detector on a third generation scanner will cause errors in angular position - resulting in a circular artifact. Fixing Ring Artifact - Recalibrate your dinosaur detector, or replace the broken part. Helical Artifact in the Axial Plane: Single Section You get the same artifacts with helical scanning modes plus you get some additional artifact from helical interpolation. The main place you see this is around the top of the skull (anatomy changing rapidly in the Z direction). The higher the pitch the worse this is. To minimize these artifacts you have to reduce the variation in the Z direction - use a low pitch, use 180 degree instead of 360 when possible, and using thin sections instead of thick. Testable trivia - this is why head CTs are still commonly done with axial scanning over helical. Helical Artifact in Multi-Section Distortion is mo…
