^ CHAPTER 8: ^ k . u iltr m ©u » - PROMETHEUS UONHART, M.D. - Sound is not light. Sound requires a medium to travel in. It’s best to think of sound as a mechanical energy that produces vibrations when propagating through material. These vibrations produce alternating areas of: High Pressure (compression) and Low Pressure (Rarefaction). (Compression) Frequency: This is the rate of change between compression and rarefaction - given in Hertz. In other words, its the number of times the wave oscillates through a cycle each second. Wavelength: The distance between areas of compression. Remember this from general chemistry? Speed = Wavelength x Frequency Low Pressure (Rarefaction) High Pressure Wavelength Frequency 180

Speed is thought of as being constant (in a particular medium), so that an increase in frequency decreases the wavelength - and vice versa. Speed in Different Materials: This is based on the compressibility of something. Things that are very compressible (air) will have a very low speed. Things that are not very compressible (bone) will have a very fast speed. The ultrasound machine is stupid - and just assumes everything travels at 1540 m/s in tissue. The fact that the machine thinks speed is constant is a source of artifacts (discussed later). Speeds effect on frequency - None. The frequency is the same, irrelevant of the sound speed in various media. Therefore the wavelength changes in media. Wave Interference Patterns: Modem ultrasound machines make a bunch of different sound beams. These sound beams can either have “constructive effects” and help each other - increase the amplitude,…

Relative Intensity - The dB. A change of 10 in the dB scale corresponds to two orders of magnitude (100 times) and so forth. The dB is based on a log 10 scale. Reducing the sound intensity to 10% is -10 dB Reducing to 1% is - 20 dB Reducing to 0.1 % is - 30 dB Testable Point: A loss of 3 dB (-3 dB) represents a 50% loss of signal intensity (power). Testable Point: The tissue thickness that reduces the ultrasound intensity by 3 dB is considered the "half-value" thickness. 182

In teractio n s of U ltrasound w ith M a tte r Ultrasound interacts with matter by: Reflection Refraction Scattering • Absorption Reflection: Ultrasound energy gets reflected at a boundary between two tissues because of the differences in the acoustic impedances of the two tissues. A large difference in “stiffness” results in a large reflection of energy. Sound Wave Reaches an Interface between two similar tissues. Sound Wave Reaches an Interface between two different tissues. Impedance: This is defined as: Z = Density x speed of sound. People like to compare this to the compressibility of a spring. At a muscle-air interface, nearly 100% of incident intensity is reflected. Ever noticed how air has some serious shadowing? This is why gel must be used between the transducer and the skin to eliminate air pockets. Small Difference in “Impedance” Results in a Small Reflection Large Difference…

Refraction: “Bending o f the sound wave caused by a change in speed” This is the change in direction of transmitted ultrasound energy at a tissue boundary when the beam is not perpendicular to said boundary. Remember the frequency doesn’t change, but the speed might. What influences refraction ? Two Things: (1) Speed Change - which is based on tissue compression, and (2) the Angle of Incidence. This was described by some dude named Snell: No refraction occurs if the speed of sound is the same in the two media or with perpendicular incidence. In other words if one side of the equation (the angle side or the speed side) is 1/1 there will be no change. Sin A n g le 1 Sin A n g le 2 S p eed 2 S p eed 1 If it hits straight on, part of the beam will bounce straight back and part of it goes straight through. If it strikes at an angle, part will be reflected and the other part will be refracted …

Scattering: There are two main categories of reflectors: • Specular (smooth) - The reflector dimensions are much larger than the wavelength of the incident. *Strength o f reflection is highly angle dependent. • Non-specular (diffuse) - The scattering surfaces are about the size of a wavelength or smaller. * Angle has no effect on strength With higher frequency ultrasound beams, the wavelength becomes smaller and the boundary no longer appears smooth from the perspective of the small wavelength. In this case, returning echoes are diffusely scattered leaving only a small fraction of the incident intensity returning to the source. Truly smooth reflections are relatively independent of frequency. Smooth Reflectors: Will Reflect Depending on the Angle of Incidence Non-Specular: Will Scatter Everywhere - Not giving a fuck about the Angle of Incidence Breaking it Down: High Frequency = Small wa…

Ahsorption: Sound energy gets turned into heat. This increases with frequency. “Attenuation ” is the loss of intensity of the ultrasound beam from both absorption and scattering in the medium. The degree of attenuation varies widely depending on the type of tissue involved. To quantify the degree of attenuation, people who like math decided to express it in dB as proportional to frequency. If you are forced into a math problem, the rule of thumb for "soft tissue" is 0.5 dB per cm per MHz or 0.5 (dB/cm)/MHz. It is proportional: • A 2-MHz ultrasound beam will have twice the attenuation of a 1-MHz beam; • A 10-MHz beam will have ten times the attenuation per unit distance. It is logarithmic: • The beam intensity is exponentially attenuated with distance. Testable Point: Half value thickness (HVT): This is the thickness of tissue necessary to attenuate the incident intensity by 50%, which is…

Review Time What determines the strength of the echoes? Angle and Impedance What is Impedance? The degree of stiffness in a tissue. The differences in tissue impedance (stiffness) determines the strength of surface reflection. What is the unit used for impedance? The Rayl You will get a big reflection if? There is a large difference in impedance. Example skin and air - thats why you gotta lube it up (gel) otherwise you can’t transmit any sound. Is the speed of sound constant in tissue? Nope - changes (via wavelength) depending on the compressibility of the tissue (slow in air, fast in bone). Thats why you have “hearing bones” in your middle ear. How does the machine know what the speed is in the various tissues the sound has traveled through? It doesn’t. It just assumes it’s always 1540 m/s - which can lead to artifacts. What makes the sound have “bend”? Changes in the speed of sound - w…

Ultrasound Transducers and Related Trivia Ultrasound is produced and received with a transducer. The device works by taking electricity and running it through a crystal (which vibrates) to convert it into mechanical energy (ultrasound waves). On the way back, the waves (mechanical energy) vibrate the crystal and turn back into electricity so they can be recorded. Or something like that... close enough for what you need to know. Back in the stone ages these things had a single element resonance crystal, now they have a broadband transducer array of hundreds of individual elements. Piezoelectric Materials: This is a crystal (or ceramic) and is the functional component of the transducer. It could be quartz, but is usually lead-zinc-titanate (PZT). The magic of the material is a well-defined molecular arrangement of electrical dipoles. When mechanically compressed, their normally organized a…

Dampening Block: This thing sits behind the crystal and absorbs the backward directed US energy. It also dampens the transducer vibration to create a pulse with a short spatial pulse length - needed to preserve detail along the beam axis (axial resolution). The process of dampening introduces a broadband frequency spectrum. What you need to know about dampening blocks: The differences in a thin block and a fat block easily lend themselves to multiple choice questions. Thin Block Thick Block v Called a "Light Damping" Called a "High Q" Has a Long Spatial Pulse Length Has a Narrow Bandwidth _ Called a "Heavy Damping" Called a "Low Q" Has a Short Spatial Pulse Length Has a Broad Bandwidth High Yield Trivia: Low Damping (high Q) - Narrow Bandwidth - For Doppler, to preserve velocity information. • Heavy Damping (low Q) - Broad Bandwidth - Gives you high spatial (axial) resolution *fewer inte…

Transducer Arrays Broadly you can have Linear (which include curved) or Phased Arrays. Linear arrays are sequenced, whereas phased arrays are “activation / reactivation” types. Linear (Sequenced) - Each element is it’s own man. They fire and receive on their own - not giving a fuck what their neighbors are doing. There is no use of interference patterns / steering on any of that fancy shit. Trivia - The Width of the Transducer = The Width of the Sum of the Individual Elements Trivia - Linear Arrays are good for Peds, and good for superficial things (carotids, leg veins, testicles, thyroids). Curvilinear - This is still a “linear” probe, meaning that it still operates with individual elements firing on their own. The difference is the face is curved. Why curved? Scan lines diverge deeper into the image. This gives you a wider field of view for deeper structures. Used in ABD imaging. Phase…

Beam Properties There are two distinct beam components: a converging beam, which narrows out to a distance determined by the geometry and frequency of the transducer (near field), and a diverging beam (far field). The Near Field (Fresnel Zone): The convergence of the near field occurs because of the multiple constructive and destructive interference patterns. Beam intensity varies from: MAX-to-min- “Normal” Probe to-MAX in a converging beam. The near field length is dependent on: Transducer Frequency and Transducer High Frequency, Large Probe Diameter. Testable Point: •Higher Transducer Frequency = Longer Near Field •Larger Diameter Element = Longer Near Field The Far Field (Fraunhofer Zone) - This is where the beam diverges. As the beam diverges the ability to distinguish two objects close to one another is reduced. The divergence is less with a high frequency big probe and more with a …

Focal Depth / Zone - The point at which the beam is at its narrowest and the area of maximum intensity. It’s the spot between the converging and diverging beams. You get your best echoes here and you get your best lateral resolution here. If you tried to image this round lesion in the far field - you would get shitty resolution because your field is diverging. If you stick the round lesion in the focal zone - you are going to maximize your beam density and get your best “lateral resolution” or “side-to-side” resolution. “Focusing” - This is a concept where you try and converge / narrow the ultrasound beam, again with the goal of improving the lateral resolution. Your two main strategies for focusing are (1) Mechanical, and (2) Electronic. Mechanical - By using a concave face you can set a fixed focal depth / zone. You do the same thing by slapping a concave acoustic lens on the front. El…

The “Stand Off Pad” Scanning very superficial things is made much easier with a “stand off pad.” WTF is a “stand o ff pa d” ? - It’s basically a block of ultrasound gel - or something with low impedance that you can scan through. As a rare piece of practical knowledge (low yield for boards) if you have a burn patient or someone with very painful skin you can submerge their hand / foot in water and use the water as a standoff pad. That way you don’t have to touch their sensitive skin / wound with the ultrasound probe. Also works great in Peds - even sick kids like water. Try that shit sometime - tell your attending you thought of it and blow their mind - they will probably be so impressed that they will have you write it up (with their name on it of course). Why does it work? It works by moving things in the superficial region (the near zone) into the focal zone. Remember lateral resoluti…

Spatial Resolution - There are three dimensions in US • Axial Lateral • Elevation (Slice Thickness) Axial Resolution: The ability to tell two closely spaced objects apart in the direction of the beam. For optimal axial resolution you need the returning echoes to be separate and not overlap. The minimum required separation between two reflectors is lA the spatial pulse length (SPL), otherwise the returning echoes will overlap. What is this “Spatial pulse length ” ? This is the number of cycles emitted per pulse by the transducer multiplied by the wavelength. Objects closer than XA Spatial Pulse Length will NOT be resolved. Lets Say This is the SPL Scenario A: M2 the length on the SPL is larger than the distance between those two nodules. Therefore they will look like 1 Big Nodule to the Scanner. In other words, you won’t be able to resolve them in the axial plane Scenario B: 112 the lengt…

Lateral Resolution: The ability of the system to resolve objects in a direction perpendicular to the beam direction. If you want to tell things apart, they must fit into separate beams. The thinner the beam, the more likely that each will fall into a separate beam. You can reason that improving lateral resolution has a lot to do with making your beam thinner. The beam is most skinny at the end of the near field (at the focal zone). Lateral resolution is worst in areas close to and far from the transducer surface (away from the focal zone). Is lateral resolution constant at different depths? No, Since the beam diameter varies with the distance from the transducer in the near and far field, the lateral resolution is depth-dependent. The best lateral resolution occurs at the focal zone (the interface of the near field and far field). Lateral resolution worsens in the deeper field. Is axial …

Scan Density If you think about the probe working by sending a series (a bunch) o f individual scan lines out and then receiving data, it should be intuitive that more lines = more information and therefore better spatial resolution. Less Scan Lines Machine thinks it s only one More Scan Lines (“more density”) *Machine thinks it s two Higher Scan Line Density = Better Lateral Resolution So what is the relationship between transducer width and lateral spatial resolution? Remember I said that lateral spatial resolution changes with depth? And that it’s best in the focal zone (shitty up close and far away)? The length o f the near field varies depending on the diameter o f the transducer (single element or function total). Anything that effects the focal zone - effects the lateral spatial resolution. Longer Transducer^ Longer Near Field Length *and longer focal zone length Shorter Transduce…

Elevation Resolution (Slice Thickness) - It’s the same as lateral resolution but is measured in the plane orthogonal to the image plane. This is usually the worst measure of resolution. It depends on the transducer element height. This type of resolution is why you can get volume averaging of acoustic details in regions that are close to the transducer and in the far field. Things to Know: • It’s similar to lateral resolution but in the orthogonal plane • It’s typically the weakest link • It depends on the height of the transducer elements • It’s a source of volume averaging before and after the focal zone (in the near and far fields) 197

S patial Resolution Sum m ary Improving Axial R esolution Improving Lateral R esolution In on R esolution Shorter Pulses (Smaller Spatial Pulse Length) Put the thing you want to look at in the focal zone. Phased array with multiple focal zones Use a fixed focal length across the entire surface of the array (downside is partial volume effects) Greater Damping “Low 0 ” (shorter pulses) Increasing the “line density” or lines per cm. Use a Thinner Crystal Minimize slice thickness - done by phase excitation of the outer to inner arrays Higher Frequency Probe (shorter wavelength) Higher Frequency Probe (less beam spreading) Dependent On Axial Resolution Spatial Pulse Length Lateral Resolution Transducer Element Width Elevation Resolution Transducer Element Height 198

Artifacts Ultrasound display equipment relies on multiple assumptions to assign the location and intensity of each received echo. When these assumptions are violated, you get an artifact. Assumptions: • All echoes originate from within the main beam • All echoes return to the transducer after a single 180 degree reflection • The amount of time an echo takes to return to the probe depends directly on the object depth. • The speed of sound in human tissue is constant - 1540 m/s • The sound beam and its echo travel in a straight path • Acoustic energy is uniformly attenuated Beam Related Artifacts: As stated above, there is an assumption that the detected echoes originate from within the main US beam. Side Lobe Artifact: The normal US beam looks like a bowtie, with a central main beam and off axis low energy beams on the side. These off axis beams are the “side lobes.” You get these side lo…

Beam Width Artifact: The US beam first exits the transducer with the same width as the transducer. It then narrows to the focal zone and then begins to diverge in the far field. The beam will eventually diverge out past the original margins of the transducer. If this diverged beam encounters a strong reflector, it could send a signal back - which will be assumed to be from the main beam (within the normal width of the transducer) and will be erroneously displayed as such. The bladder is the classic place to show this, with peripheral echoes. A good “next step” question would be to show this artifact and have you improve it by (1) adjusting the focal zone to the level of interest and (2) placing the transducer at the center o f the image. Artifacts Associated with Multiple Echoes: These artifacts violate the assumption that an echo returns to the transducer after a single reflection and t…

Mirror Image Artifact: Like other artifact in this category, this is created by the false assumption that an echo returns to the transducer after a single reflection. In this situation the ultrasound beam passes through a highly reflective surface, then gets repeatedly reflected between the back side of the reflector and the adjacent structure. This is displayed as a duplication equidistant from but deep to the strongly reflective interface. The classic location is along the liver / lung interface as shown - with liver parenchyma where you should have lung. Artifacts from Multiple Echoes Reverberation Two parallel highly reflective surfaces - Multiple equidistantly spaced linear reflections. Comet Tail Two parallel highly reflective surfaces - closer together (< 1/2 SPL) Triangle (comet) shaped Ring Down Artifact Fluid trapped between a tetrahedron of air bubbles Parallel band extending …

Artifacts Related to Velocity Errors: The assumption is that the speed of sound is 1540 m/s in human tissue. Sometimes the beam encounters media (air, fluid , fat, bone) where this doesn’t hold up exactly. If it travels slower, it takes longer to return and the machine thinks the depth is more. If it travels faster, it comes back to the monitor faster and the machine thinks the depth is less. Speed Displacement Artifact: The speed of sound slows down in fat, relative to liver. This means the beam takes longer to return and is perceived by the machine as being further away. This creates the appearance of a discontinuous and focally displaced liver border. Speed Displacement Artifact Refraction Artifact: As discussed earlier in the US section, speed difference in tissues causes refraction (as described by Snell). This change in the direction of the sound wave violates the idea that the bea…

Artifacts Related to Attenuation Errors: Ultrasound beams are attenuated as they travel through the body (from scatter and absorption). The longer the echo travels, the more it will be attenuated (relative to an echo which traveled a shorter distance). ‘‘Compensation Amplification ” - This is a type of processing that ultrasound machines use on echoes which take longer to return to the transducer. Essentially, the echoes that return later are amplified more than echoes that return earlier. The end result is an image that appears more uniform in the deep field. Shadowing - If the ultrasound beam runs into a material that attenuates sound to a larger degree than the surrounding tissue, the strength of the beam distal to this structure appears weaker (darker) than in the surrounding field. Increased Through Transmission - This is the opposite of shadowing. Instead, the beam runs into materi…

Doppler The idea is that the frequency of sound changes with moving objects. This is usually explained by thinking about a car blowing it’s horn moving towards you (or away from you). Basically the sound wave gets crowded at the front, and will sound different depending on where you are standing in relationship to the moving object (origin of the sound wave). Understanding it really isn’t that important. It’s a thing.. .and because it’s a thing you can measure these changes (or shifts) in frequency to tell what direction things are moving (and how fast they are getting there). 204

D oppler A n g le: In an ideal world, the sample volume would be placed in the mid-part of the lumen, with an angle of incidence parallel to the vessel. If this angle is placed incorrectly the velocity calculation will be incorrect (especially if the angle is greater than 60). The angle should be between 30- 60. Why less than 60? Because of the formula for calculating it. Here let’s have a look at it, and it should make perfect sense. To make it less confusing I’ve written it in Egyptian Hieroglyphs, why would I do that? Because the only part of it you need to understand is the “Cosine” part. You can imagine if cosine was zero (which occurs at 90 degrees), then the numerator (top part) would be zero.... and zero over something is zero. A shift of zero would make you think the blood isn’t Dop = ■ < ] (C o s 0 > 3 ^ ) ' / V A How does the change in angle - change the Cosine? Because of the…

Pulsed Wave (Spectral) Doppler- This utilizes a single transducer for both reception and transmission. Blood flow velocity varies yielding a spectrum of doppler shifts instead of a single frequency. You can obtain the direction of blood flow (plotted above and below the baseline) and velocity. Color Doppler: This uses the gray scale image with a superimposed color blood flow image. Gives the direction of flow, this time with colors (red and blue). Color intensity varies depending on the flow intensity (things holding still are gray). This type of doppler obtains samples of each pixel multiple times and then displays the average shift. Testable Trivia: The spatial resolution is less compared to gray scale imaging (although smaller vessels are better seen). Testable Trivia: The doppler angle is not as important since the information is semi- quantitative. Power Doppler: Power doppler is di…

Doppler A rtifacts: Aliasing - Super high velocities are displayed as low (negative). On a spectral system it looks like the spectrum is cut off and “wrapped around the baseline”, reappearing on the opposite end. Testable Trivia: This artifact occurs when the doppler shift is greater than a threshold called the “Nyquist frequency” Nyquist limit (kHz) = 1/2 x pulse repetition frequency (PRF) Example: A frequency shift of 3.5 kHz requires a PRE of 7 kHz to avoid aliasing. Aliasing can be reduced or eliminated by: Decreasing Doppler shift - by either using a lower frequency transducer or using a doppler angle closer to 90 (increasing the angle) • Increasing the Pulse Repetition Frequency (which will increase your Nyquist) or selecting a sample volume at a lesser depth or increasing the scale. Tissue Vibration: This occurs secondary to turbulent blood flow. The doppler shows a mixture of red…

Image Optimization This vs That: Output Power vs Receiver Gain: Output Power (‘‘Transmit Gain) - Cranking this knob up increases the brightness by adjusting the strength of the sound pulse SENT TO THE BODY by the transducer. When the image is too bright from high output power, this w ill degrade lateral resolution (think about it widening the beam). Receiver Gain - Cranking this knob up increases brightness by adjusting the strength of the sound pulse AFTER IT RETURNS to the transducer. Think about this like a post-processing technique. • BOTH CAN LIGHTEN or DARKEN THE ENTIRE IMAGE. ~ If Forced to Choose ALARA favors Receiver Gain is a first option TGC - Time Gain Compensation This is the button you push to make the image look better. What it actually does is progressively increase the amount of amplification applied with depth. The man who lives in the ultrasound machine (“computer” I c…

Harmonics -A Promethean Dialogue- A lot of discussion in ultrasound physics centers around how the sound changes as it goes through the body, and the various artifacts and such that result (so on and so forth, etc..ect..) - and there will be some of that in this dialogue, but first we need to have another look at the machine. Specifically, we should to focus on how the sonographic system is going to take received signal and make a diagnostic image. A basic way to look at it is like this: ^ Beam L Signal j L Image Former ♦ — As sound waves return to the probe the “Beam Former” will amplify and combine the contributions from the various of individual elements. The next step is the “Signal Processor. ” The “Signal Processor” will do several things - but the main thing relevant for this discussion is the task of filtering out undesirable noise. The way this works is the probe knows what freq…

Before we discuss why you might want to do this - let us discuss how harmonic frequencies are generated in tissues (who does #2 work for ?) You can think about sound waves as area of compression (high density) and rarefaction (low density) occurring in repeating cycles. The areas of high density (compression) correspond with the positive direction of the wave or “high pressure” and the areas of low density correspond with the negative direction of the wave or “low pressure.” The reason I bring this up, is that as the wave travels through a medium (i.e. the cirrhotic liver of an IV drug user who is salivating at the chance to sue you), it doesn’t do so uniformly. The high pressure portion will travel faster than the low pressure portion. As this happens the shape of the wave changes, eventually forming even and odd “harmonics” from the original (fundamental) wave. 1st Harm onic 2nd Harmon…

There are 3 primary advantages to doing this: (1) Improved Lateral Resolution. The reason for the improvement in lateral resolution is that the harmonic beam more narrow. Fundamental Beam with 2nd Harmonic Just the 2nd Harmonic (notice it is skinner) Remember that Lateral Resolution is limited by the WIDTH of the pulse. People will often write that as “beam width” rather than pulse width - but we are talking about the same thing since “beam” is just a series of identical pulses given off in rapid succession. Small beam width- reflectors close together are still resolved. Machine correctly identifies two objects Large beam width- reflectors close together are NOT resolved. Machine thinks it is just one reflector NARROWER BEAM WIDTH = BETTER LATERAL RESOLUTION 211

W hat about Axial Resolution ? Remember that axial resolution is limited by the length of the ultrasound pulse - the “Spatial Pulse Length” or “SPL,” ? Axial Resolution = Spatial Pulse Length / 2. When the bandwidth (Bw) is narrowed (via filtering out the fundamental frequency) you are going to lengthen the pulse length. Pulse Length = 1/ Bw Shorter SPL: PL = 1/Bw 1 st Harmonic Summary: Harmonics have a shorter bandwidth. This results in a longer spatial pulse length. Longer spatial pulse length = reduced axial resolution. Trivia: The solution to this problem is a technique called “pulse inversion.” The nuts and bolts of how “pulse inversion” works is probably beyond the scope of the exam. Probably... Before you spaz out and start googling it consider this - if the difference between you passing and failing this test comes down to knowing the inner mechanics of obscure ultrasound algorit…

OK... so I said there were 3 Advantages. The first was IMPROVED LATERAL RESOLUTION. Then we had a brief tangent, so I could point out that harmonics can actually reduce axial resolution. Now - to finish up with these last two potential advantages. (2) Grating Lobe A rtifacts are Reduced / Elim inated. I guess before I get into why they are eliminated I should answer the burning question. “What the fuck are grating lobe artifacts ” ? The simplest explanation is that they represent off-axis sound. Normally the beam shoots straight down. However, sound energy can be emitted far off-axis (not on purpose - it just happens sometimes) - and these things are called “side lobes” or “grating lobes” - and the difference between those is two words is meaningless — seriously, don’t waste time going there. Main Beam Usually the presence of a grating lobe doesn’t mean shit because they aren’t strong en…

(3) Superficial Reverberation is Reduced / Elim inated. There are several reasons why this occurs. The first one is depth. Remember Harmonics are generated deep in the tissue (it takes time from the high pressure - low pressure differences to morph the wave). As a result artifacts that are generated in the superficial tissues — for example reverberation between ribs will be removed. ~ RIB Reverberations - from strong reflector (ribs) Fundamental Beam with 2nd Harmonic NO Reverberations RIB Just the 2nd Harmonic - notice it is skinner Another reason is the energy of the reflected sound. As a quick refresher, reverberations occur when the sound beam is repeatedly reflected between two interfaces with a high acoustic impedance mismatch (difference). So you get a strong bounce back to the transducer face. At that point the computer who lives inside the probe recognizes that first echo and lo…

Harm onics Rapid R eview & Sum m ary As the ultrasound beam travels into the patient’s tissues the transmitted pulse is progressively distorted. However this occurs primarily in the central zone of the main beam where the intensity is high. A distorted pulse will give rise to distorted echoes and these have significant energy at harmonic frequencies. Tissue harmonics are made using the second harmonic component of the echo signal, with the fundamental frequency excluded. Undistorted echoes coming from lower intensity areas (fringes of the beam, side lobes, superficial tissues) are not seen. The beam changes as it passes through tissues The receiver can be set to accept this altered frequency What you need to know: • Harmonics involves transmitting at one frequency and receiving at another - “the second harmonic” they call it. • Possible because body tissues cause some distortion of the w…

' - ,y ; m - Compound Im aging - Having the electronic steering of the ultrasound beams from the transducer image an object in multiple different directions. This will sharpen the edges and cause loss of posterior shadowing (can make a cyst look solid). What you need to know: • Compound imaging uses electronic steering of the ultrasound beams from the transducer to image an object in multiple different directions. At least this is how spatial compound imaging works (there are other more complex mechanisms but - it doesn’t matter... probably). • Will sharpen the edges • Will cause loss of posterior shadowing Focal Zone Normal Scanning Compound Imaging (Peaking around the comers) Remember to optimize an image you want to drop your focal zone, narrowed appropriately, over the object of interest. WRONG! - Focal Zone Centered Too Low WRONG! - Focal Zone Too Wide CORRECT - Focal Zone Centered …

This vs That - Harmonics vs Compound Imaging These toys are great, but sometimes they cause problems. The classic way to illustrate this is to think about a breast “nodule” in mammo. Normal Hypoechoic but not totally anechoic Some Shadowing - but not a ton Blurry Margins Reverberation Artifact Harmonics Looks more hypoechoic or almost anechoic Shadowing tends to be worse Blurry Margins stay Reverberation Artifact, side lobe / grating and “speckle are GONE Compound Hypoechoic but not totally anechoic Shadowing is GONE Margins are SHARP Harmonics can make a solid thing looks cystic (by turning it more anechoic). This can sometimes make it easier to see a “nodule” on the background breast parenchyma. Just make sure you flip it on and off to make sure it’s not a cyst. From a practical standpoint this is most useful in finding a lesion that has similar echotexture to the background, biopsy ta…

Safety First let us discuss some vocabulary related to Amplitude: This is the difference between the average value of the waveform. This is not constant throughout the beam. If you cut the beam in cross section you’d see that it’s more intense in the center, and less on the edges. I bring this up because it illustrates the need to discuss “intensity.” Intensity = power / area. • If the beam power increases the intensity increases. • If the beam area decreases (which happens during focusing) the intensity increases. • If you square the amplitude you quadruple the intensity. Some other important vocabulary words: • Spatial Peak Intensity = The greatest intensity found in the beam • Temporal Average Intensity = The average intensity over pulse times and listening times. Spatial Peak Spatial Average — Temporal Peak Temporal A verage how strong / intense an ultrasound beam is. peak and the Am…

Relative Intensity of Modes Remember the smaller the beam area the greater the intensity. Intensity = Power / Area. This is the easiest way to keep these straight - from a conceptual point of view. “B-Mode ” - Also known as “brightness mode” or “2D mode” - This is the standard grey scale mode that you use all the time. The field of view is fairly large, i.e. a big square. “M-Mode ” - This is more of a cardiac ECHO thing (although you can also use it for fetal HR). This mode focuses on the ability to see “motion” - for example a heart valve. It uses rapidly emitted pulses over a narrow beam to determine the velocity of that specific structure in question. Think about it as zeroing in on a very small structure to see if it is moving. Smaller beam = more intensity. “Doppler” - Think about the Doppler sample as occurring over a tiny little line - which is necessary to see those little shifts…

Two acoustic output parameters are used as indicators of the potential for biologic effect. •Thermal Index (TI) - this is the maximum temperature rise in tissue secondary to energy absorption. This is based on a homogeneous tissue model with certain instrument parameters. •Mechanical Index (MI) -this is how likely it is that cavitation will occur considering peak rarefaction pressure and frequency. This index is the indicator of mechanical bioeffects (streaming and cavitation). This matters the most with contrast enhanced US. Cavitation - Sonically-generated activity in compressible bodies composed of gas and/or vapor. This can be sub-classified as either stable or transient. •Stable Cavitation - Micro bubbles are already present in the media. They expand and contract as the waves cycle (responding to pressure). This occurs at low and intermediate ultrasound intensities (as used clinical…

Obstetrics: The bottom line is ultrasound has been around for fucking ever, and there has never ever ever been a single case of a human fetus injured by it. Having said that, Radiologists are massive cowards so caution is still advised. Since temperature rises are most likely at bone surfaces and adjacent soft tissues, a baby with increasing mineralization (2nd and 3rd trimester) has the theoretical risk of the possibility of heating sensitive tissues such as brain and spinal cord. Sensitive tissues • Embryo less than eight weeks after conception • Head, brain or spine of any fetus or neonate • Eyeball (in a subject of any age). Nuclear medicine is unique in that instead of passing a beam of energy through the patient, you are injecting a tracer into the patient and observing a normal or abnormal distribution of that tracer. Stability / Decay: Everyone wants to be happy. I want to be happy, you want to be happy, and even isotopes want to be happy. We seek it in different ways. Some of us incorrectly thought that going to medical school would make us happy. Isotopes seek happiness through stability. There is this thing called the “band o f stability, ” that demonstrates the happy place atoms go to when they have a certain balance of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. It’s not exactly 1:1, but it’s pretty close. Neutrons “N” Protons = Neutrons (1:1) Beta Plus (Positron) or Electron Capture P > N Protons “Z” 224

As you can see from the chart depending on the imbalance of neutrons / protons different types of decay occur. Situation 1: “Lots of N eutrons, Not Enough Protons” Beta Minus Decay: The solution to the problem is to take one of your neutrons (you’ve got extra) and turn it into a proton. This change from a neutron to a proton results in the emission of a “beta particle” from the nucleus. WTF is a “Beta Particle ” ? - It’s basically an electron. Why not just call it an electron? Because “Real Electrons” don’t come from the nucleus. This pansy ass electron is a “beta male” electron - being Why is an electron (beta particle) being ejected? There are two reasons. (1) You need to change the charge of a neutron from neutral to positive. It’s best to think about neutrons as being 1/2 negative and 1/2 positive. If you eject the negative part, then you become all positive. The second reason (2) is…

Situation 2a: “Lots of Protons, Not Enough Neutrons” There are two situations that can occur when you have too many protons and not enough neutrons, and just like in real life your choices depend on how much money you have. “The Rich Guy” - Beta Positive Decay It’s no big deal for the Rich Guy to buy a Ferrari or take an expensive vacation. It’s also no big deal for him to undergo beta positive decay. Why? Because he has 1.02 MeV and has no problem blowing it. Why does he have no problem blowing it? Because he didn’t work for it - his Daddy gave it to him. So here is how it goes down: A positively charged proton is converted into a neutral neutron. How does it do that? By giving up it’s positive charge in the form of a “positron” Just like in beta minus decay an addition POS neutrino is also emitted just to keep the energy balanced. What is this “Positron ” ? It’s basically an “anti-elec…

Situation 2b: “Lots of Protons, Not Enough Neutrons” “The Poor Guy” - Electron Capture If you weren’t bom with a silver spoon in your mouth it may be necessary to steal (capture) to be happy. Remember you are trying to change a positively charged proton into a neutral neutron. The rich guy had enough money (1.02 MeV) to bribe the positively charged part of the proton to leave. The poor guy doesn’t have that option. So instead of subtracting a positive from a positive to make a neutral - the strategy here is to ADD a negative to a positive to make a neutral. How do you pull this off? An electron is lured into the nucleus from the K shell. I imagine the Nucleus says something like “do you like candy?... I have some in the nucleus.” What kind o f a process is this (what fancy general chemistry vocabulary word) ? This process is an ISOBAR1C transition. Remember, just like Beta Minus, in Beta…

T e ll M e M o re A b o u t th is “Is o m e ric T ra n s itio n ” After undergoing an isobaric transition (beta emission, positron emission, or electron capture), there is often left-over energy. This energy needs to be emitted prior to achieving final stability. One process for achieving this final stability is isomeric transition - i.e. emitting energy and dropping to the ground state. Gamma Emission: In most isomeric transitions, a nucleus will emit its excess energy in the form of a gamma photon. The energy of these photons is variable and depends on the energy differences between the intermediate and final states of the nucleus undergoing isomeric transition. Although the TOTAL energy emission for a given nuclei of a specific nuclide is the same, many times you have more than one intermediate state - with multiple different energy gamma photons.. This is why you see multiple peaks f…

Tell Me More About this “Isomeric Transition” - Continued Nemesis - Internal Conversion Gamma emission has an arch nemesis called “Internal Conversion”. The details of this probably aren’t super important so I won’t go into excruciating detail: just know that under certain conditions, the energy you would normally get rid of via gamma emission can be transferred to an electron within the atom. This transfer of energy is more than the binding energy and will expel the electron. The vacancy will lead to a downward cascade from a higher level - and result in either the emission of a characteristic x-ray or an Auger electron. Characteristic X-Ray Emission Bad because you are emitting particles - which can cause harm, not imageable gamma photons which contribute to the study. Augur Electron Emission Alpha Decay For Completeness - Alpha Decay = This tends to occur in heavier unstable atoms. Al…

Production There are two main ways you can create tracers, both of which alter the Neutron/Proton ratio to make them unstable: (1) Bombardment (in a nuclear reactor or cyclotron) (2) Fission Bombardment: This is basically striking target elements with either neutrons (in a nuclear reactor), or with charged particles (alpha particles, protons, or deuterons) in a cyclotron. The downside to the nuclear reactor method is that you have to clean up the leftover parent element. This is referred to as not being carrier free. The cyclotron has the advantage of producing elements via transmutation, therefore you don’t have any parents to clean up -aka “Carrier Free.” -----------------------------— ......... ...... “Neutrons" I Nudeai Reacloi Example: 9842Mo + Neutron ^ " 42M 0 + Gamma This usually results in transmutation, so you don’t have to clean up the parent. -CARRIER FREE Fission: Neutrons a…

Radioactive Decay “Half Life” - Which usually means the physical half-life. The amount of time necessary for a radionuclide to be reduced to half of its existing activity. In addition to physical half life, you have biologic half life. The biologic half life is basically how long it takes to shit or piss half the tracer out. The “effective half life” takes both of these things into consideration. Let’s do a couple o f practice questions: 1) The nukes department has a supply o f 10 Ci o f 99mTc at 8:00 AM. The patient is showing up the next day at 8:00 AM the following morning — how much activity will be left? To answer this question you need to know the half life of the agent. They are NOT going to tell you that (see the list at the end of this chapter & book). The second thing you’ll need to do is figure out how many half lives are going down in the time period in question. The half lif…

“Activity” is defined by the amount of disintegrations per second. Historically this was measured in Curie (Ci) which is 3.7 xlO10disintegrations per second. The new SI unit is the Becquerel (Bq), which is one disintegration per second. “Specific Activity” is defined as the activity per unit of mass (Bq/g). The longer the half-life, the lower its specific activity. How the Gamma Camera Works: A gamma camera takes photons emitted from the radionuclide, turns it into a light pulse, and then takes that light pulse and makes some voltage. The voltage makes a picture. I Collimator Crystal Photomultiplier Computer 233

Collim ator: There are a lot of unwanted photons (background, off-axis, Compton scatter, etc...). The purpose of the collimator is to reduce scatter and allow for correct localization of radionuclide events. It works by discriminating based on direction of travel. It can NOT tell the difference in photon energy (that’s done by the photon height analyzer). Parallel Hole Collimator: “The Work Horse” This can be modified based on the energy of the tracer being used. Low Energy (l-200keV)- Thinner Plates: 99mTc, 1231, I33Xe, 201 TI Medium Energy (200-400keV): 67Ga, 111 In High Energy (> 400keV) - Thicker Plates: 1311 (technically most energy peaks are medium) Sensitivity uv Resolution: These two guys have an inverse relationship. As one goes up the other goes down. A high sensitivity collimator will allow twice as many counts to be imaged but will degrade the spacial resolution. High sensiti…

Resolution Pearls: Pinhole Collimator: Magnifies and inverts image. Used for thyroids and other small parts. It’s usually cone shaped. Magnification occurs at a ratio of: pinhole to detector “F” / pinhole to patient “B” If F = B there is no magnification If F > B = there is magnification If F < B = object gets smaller. An increase in object-to- collimator distance degrades the system spatial resolution. The practical application is patient positing. The closer the detector is to the patient, the better the resolution of the images. In other words, if you move the pinhole far enough away from the patient it will make the image smaller. Magnification at the front (anterior) is greater than the back (objects are 3D), this is why large objects get distorted and pinholes work best on small things. Sensitivity for pinhole cameras is garbage. Converging Hole Collimator (Cone Beam): Holes are cl…

Scintillation Crystal- Once the photon emerges from the collimator, it impacts on the crystal. The crystal is made of sodium iodine doped with thallium. The crystal has the property that when struck with a photon it produces a pulse of light. The thicker the crystal the less photons will be wasted (just pass through). On this same principal, thin crystals will let more photons through (without generating light). Energy of the photons themselves also plays a similar role. Higher energy photons will pass right through the crystal (lower ones won’t). A thicker crystal is obviously better for high energy photons. So why not just make all the crystals super thick? Apparently you sacrifice spatial resolution with a thick crystal, because with a thinner crystal the photomultiplier tubes can sit closer to the event and more accurately localize it. * Thick crystal = Better Sensitivity, But Worse …

Key Concept: “Dow nscatter”- There is a thing called downscatter, which is super super high yield. Essentially high energy photons can spill into the window of a low energy emitter, mainly resulting from Compton scatter effects. Example 1: You are doing a V/Q scan using 133Xe and 99mTc. Xe has an energy of 8IkeV, whereas Tc has an energy of 140keV. The Tc Compton scatter is gonna range down from about 135 to 90 (as in the above diagram “from a person”). So if you inject the Tc first and image that will turn out just fine. But when you give the patient the Xe they will still have the Tc on board so you will be getting Xe peaks at 81, with a bunch of Tc Compton scatter all over it. So you totally hosed yourself, because the Pulse Height Analyzer can’t tell what is Compton scatter from the Tc and what is signal from the Xe. The solution is to image with the Xe first, because the 81 keV and …

SPECT and Matrix: Gamma camera imaging can be “static” or “dynamic.” When improved spatial resolution is needed “SPECT” can be performed by rotating a camera 180 or 360 degrees around the patient. The longer you image on SPECT the better it looks, but patients just can’t hold still forever - and movement degrades the images. Another thing to consider is matrix size. Matrix size: Size matters in most things in life, matrix being no different. A matrix size of 128x128 has superior resolution to 64x64. However, a larger matrix size means longer acquisition time (which patients hate), and reduced count density per pixel (which impacts image contrast) S tar A rtifact: If you have very focal intense energy you can sometimes see a star artifact, caused by septal penetration of the hexagonal collimator holes. This is typically seen in the thyroid bed after a high therapeutic dose (using a medium…

Quality Control for Gamma Cameras Possibly the most dreaded portion of radiology multiple choice exams (right up there with safety, and mammo MQSA). Daily, weekly, and quarterly tests are done on the camera (BY A TECH! NOT A RADIOLOGIST!!!) to make sure it’s functioning properly. As if this wasn’t bad enough, pinning down “official” recommendations is difficult as the NRC essentially says hire a medical physicist and do whatever the fuck they say. Honestly, if I was running the NRC I’d give similar advice. The problem is choosing consensus information for the purpose of multiple choice. The following is my best attempt at guessing “the right answer” for trivia that could be asked. Four parameters are usually tested for: field uniformity, window setting, image linearity and spatial resolution, and Center of Rotation (COR). Field Uniformity: So the photo multiplier tubes can have subtle va…

Energy Window: The correct window needs to be used prior to each study, therefore this test should be performed daily. The most common approach to testing this is to use a symmetric window centered at the peak energy used in the imaging test. The source can be a syringe, a vial, or if absolutely necessary the patient. For Tc you would use a 20% window centered at 140keV. Image Linearity and Spatial Resolution: Lead bar phantoms with parallel lines, placed between the collimator and a 57Co sheet, are used to test image resolution and linearity. This only needs to be done weekly. Resolution is defined by the ability to differentiate between two distinct points (can you tell the bars are separate). Linearity is tested by looking to see if all the bars are straight (some distortion at the edges is ok). The bars should NOT look wavy. Quality Control for Gamma Cameras Continued Center of Rotat…

Safety This is also an excruciating topic for Radiologists to learn about. As previously stated nukes is full of random trivia, and let’s be honest academic nuclear medicine tends to attract eccentric personalities... and they are writing the questions. Lead Aprons / Film Badges / Ring Badges Why don’t nuclear medicine techs wear lead? Two reasons: (1) Thin lead doesn’t stop gamma rays. To stop them it would have to be so heavy you couldn’t move. (2) When high energy gamma rays collide with a dense material that is not thick enough to stop them (lead apron) they rapidly slow down and lose energy which turns them into deeply penetrating Bremsstrahlung x-rays - which actually makes the dose worse. Correct Positioning on the Film Badge and Ring Badge * Film Badge: Should be worn on the collar at the chest / neck level • Ring Badge: Worn on dominant hand, index finger, label in towards sourc…

• Geiger -Muller Counter - this is a device used to detect small amounts of radioactive contamination. The device has a gas-filled chamber, which when it comes in contact with radiation becomes ionized and creates some voltage between a cathode and an anode - lots of beeping and clicking occurs. There are two ways that questions about this device are classically asked; (1) by just showing you a picture of one and saying “what is this”, (2) by testing the concept of “dead time.” What is this “dead time” ? Although the device is very sensitive, it is also vulnerable to being overloaded by a large dose of radiation. Then you have “dead time” as the ionization must dissipate before it can respond again. The maximum dose it can handle is about lOOmR/h. So if the device clicks once and stops it might be because of “dead time” (it might also be “dead time” for you). Trivia - The GM Counter can …

Device Trivia Personal Dosimeters “Pocket Ionization Detector” - uses a miniature ionization chamber. They give you real-time estimated dose, but must be charged and zero’d prior to use. These are not used anymore - which makes them high yield. “SolidState Dosimeter” - Accumulated dose or rate can be read real time with LCD display. “Film Badge” - Uses a thin metallic filter with a radiosensitive film. The degree of darkening (relative optic density) corresponds with dose. They can be damaged by temperature, humidity, etc... “Optically Stimulated Dosimeter ” - Replaced the film badge. Chips / Strips are placed under a filter. “Thermo-luminescent Dosimeter” - The ring badge. Should be worn under a glove, with the label facing the palm (target). Survey Meters G-M and Ionization Detectors - discussed above Well Counter Basically a small gamma camera, with one PMT. Susceptible to “dead time”…

Readout of activity is made in mCi. The range of most devices is 30 microCi to 2 Ci. Dose should be within 5% of computed activity, and this should be checked daily. * Consistency - should be within 5% of computed activity. Checked with reference sources (checked Daily) * Linearity - accurate readout for activities over the whole range of potentially encountered activities - checked with a large activity of Tc (around 200mCi) and decaying it down to less than the smallest activity you would measure for use. Or - the easy way - use a Calicheck© or Lineator© kit (which contains sheets of varied thickness of lead, simulating decay over time), (checked quarterly). * Accuracy - Standard measurements of radiotracers measured and compared to what the activity should be (performed at installation of the device and annually). * Geometry - Correction for different positioning and size (different v…

Regulations: No bureaucracy is complete without lots of rules and regulations (and of course agencies to police such regulations). The Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) is where you will want to look for all your rules and regulations. Specifically part 19 (inspections), part 20 (radiation protection), and part 35 (human use of radioisotopes) . The “NRC” is the governing body that has been charged with the task of enforcing all these various directives. It is possible for individual states to reach an agreement with the Federal Government to enforce these rules on their own. These are called “Agreement States”, and the main thing to know is that they can be more strict, but not less strict than the national agency. NRC Trivia: - 10 CFR part 19: Notices, instructions, and reports to workers. - 10 CFR part 20: Standards for protection against radiation. - 10 CFR part 35: Medical use of by-…

MINOR Spills (1) Protect the Patient: If a spill occurs while a patient is in distress, address the patient first. Once he/she is stable then address the spill. (2) Confine the Spill / Limit the Spread: Secure the area and make sure people aren’t tracking it all over the place. (3) Clean Up the Spill: Use gloves (wear shoes), and all other personal protective equipment including a radiation badge. If possible tongs or forceps are even better for grabbing stuff. Use a damp absorbent material to clean the spill (working from the outside to the center). (4) Survey Cleanup Items: Anything used in the clean up needs to be surveyed or presumed contaminated (held until they decay to safe levels - rule is 10 half lives). (5) Survey Cleanup People: People also need to be surveyed by the radiation safety officer (in a different area - to avoid count interference). What if ? • What if it gets on my…

Obviously it is best if the Radiation Safety Officer (RSO) is involved and doing all that stuff. In the real world that is what will happen. But the ABR feels that on an intermediate level test you should be able to perform the tasks of doctor, tech, radiation safety officer, and janitor (while receiving the salary equivalent of the janitor). Regulations Affecting the General Public Regulations demand the following: • Annual Dose limit of 100 mrem to the public • Not greater than 2 mrem per hour - in an “unrestricted area” • “Restricted Area” = Any place that receives a dose greater than 2 mrem/h Signs must be placed with the following slogans: • Radiation Area: Any place you could get 0.005 rem (0.05mSv) in 1 hour at 30cm • High Radiation Area: Any place you could get 0.1 rem (lmSv) in 1 hour at 30cm • Very High Radiation Area: Any place you could get 500 rads (5 gray) in 1 hour at 1 me…

You want to be a Radiologist? You better get your vocab straight on this stuff. Bean counters take this stuff pretty seriously: When does a medical event occur??? Well you have to meet two criteria: (1) You have to FUCK IT UP — Dude, are you fucking this up? • Wrong drug, Wrong route, Wrong patient, or Wrong dose (more than 20%). • OR Patient receives a dose to a part of the body other than the intended treatment site that exceeds by 50% or more the dose expected by proper administration and prescription (2) You have to HARM THE PATIENT: “Harm ” defined as either: A. Whole body dose > 5 rem, or B. Single organ dose > 50 rem Medical Events require you to call the doctor who ordered it, patient, and NRC/State and exp…

Receiving, Storing, and Disposing of R adioactive M aterial Within 3 hours of receipt (3 working hours) you (the tech) have to survey packages when they arrive. This process involves a GM counter test at the surface and 1 meter from the package, as well as wipes of all surfaces of the package (>6600 dpm/300 cm2 is not allowed). Keep the package in a controlled area, like the hot lab (just in case A1 Qaeda invades your hospital). Contact both the shipper and the NRC if beyond allowable limits. Package labels and allowable limits White 1: No special handling, surface dose rate < 0.5 mRem/hr, 1 meter ~ 0 mRem/hr Yellow 2: Special handling required, surface dose rate <50 mRem/hr, 1 meter < 1 mRem/hr Yellow 3: Special handling required, surface dose rate < 200 mRem/hr, 1 meter < 10 mRem/hr *Seriously, these assholes couldn’t pick another color? - instead they choose “yellow 3 ” “Transport Ind…

Tc-99 I now want to transition into discussing Tc-99. It’s the workhorse of nuclear medicine, and its production / purity is likely high yield for multiple choice. First let’s talk (read) about how Tc is made. Vacuum s ' Step 2 : Radionuclide Purity Test You want to make sure you just have Tc, and you didn’t wash any Mo off as well. Mo that is in the sample is called “break through. ” The basis for the test is to look for the different photo peaks of the radionuclides. The sample is placed behind a lead shield. Mo is assayed for first. The high energy photons of Mo (like 740 keV) will NOT be attenuated by the shield, but the 140 keV Tc photons will be. • NRC allows no more than 0.15 micro Ci of Mo per 1 milli Ci of Tc, at the time of administration. Few pitfalls here: • (I) note the unit change from micro to milli, • (2) note the “time o f administration ” * If ratio is less than 0.038 a…

Step 3: Chemical Purity Test Remember that the column is made of Aluminum Oxide, which can wash off, clump up with the Tc, and show up as liver activity, or cause sulfur colloid aggregation and show up in the lungs on liver spleen scan. The test for purity is performed with pH paper. The allowed amount is < 10 microgram A1 per 1 ml. Step 4: Radiochemical Purity Test As mentioned above, Tc comes out of the generator as Na99mTc04. So, to use it for anything useful it needs to be reduced (accomplished by adding it to SnCh). Thin layer chromatography is used to assess for purity. Limits for Free Tc * 95% Na99mTc04 * 92% for 99mTc sulfur colloid (MAA) * 91% for all other Tc radiopharmaceuticals Buckle Up Bitches: Questions can be sneaky.... It’s also a term to describe when the concentration of parent and daughter isotopes are equal. Transient Equilibrium: This type of equilibrium occurs when the half life of the daughter is a little bit shorter than the parent. The classic example is the Moly-99 generator making Tc-99. This vs T h at - C ritical Organ vs T arg et Organ: “Critical Organ” - An organ that limits the dose of the radiopharmaceutical due to the increased susceptibility of the critical organ for cancer. This may or may not be the “Target Organ.” “Target Organ”- This is the organ you want the tracer to accumulate in. It’s your organ of interest. A few generalizations on Critical Organs: If you are trying to figure it out, it’s the organ that the tracer is going to spend the most time in. For example, Gallium ends up in the bowel. Tc RBC scans are going to end up passing through the heart a lot, unless they are heat treated then the spleen eats them. Tc-MAG3s and DTPA is going to be the bladder, but DMSA (which sticks to the kidney) is going to be the kidney. Why all the Controversy ? You will read conflicting information for many of these. Apparently, there is a fair amount of bickering among …

SPECT What is this SPECT? - The simple way to think about it is like this. CT is to plain film, as SPECT is the planar. Basically you take a gamma camera(s) (pretty damn similar to the one you use for planar) and rotate them around the patient. What this does is give you a 3D look — instead of a 2D look (like you get with planar). SPECT is a single photon system - unlike PET which uses two photons (from a positron annihilation event). Just like a regular planar scan, SPECT is not fast. Each projection takes like 30 seconds and the total scan time is around 15 mins. The matrix size is usually around 128 x 128 (for things that are not cardiac). The scan uses iterative reconstruction to make a picture. Because the data is isotropic volume, reconstruction in 3 planes is possible. Sensitivity is depth dependent, with radiation from different tissue origins attenuated to different degrees. Spe…

PET An Overview: Remember, there are two situations that can occur when you have too many protons and not enough neutrons. The “Rich Guy” situation - where you have 1.02 MeV to blow - results in the emission of a positron during the conversion of a proton to a neutron. This positron is going to travel a very short distance (less than 2mm) before it meets its arch nemesis the electron. The two fight to a draw, mutually annihilating each other. The annihilation event splits that 1.02 MeV in half, creating two 511 keV photons which are emitted exactly 180 degrees from each other. PET scanners work by detecting these 511 keV photon pairs - doing lots of math to determine their location of origin. SPECT v.v PET - There are a few differences. (1) SPECT is a few cameras rotating around the patient. PET is a set of complete rings/ detectors surrounding the patient. The PET detectors are much mor…

Localization / Coincidence Detection As mentioned before, PET has a complete ring(s) around the patient. As the 511 pairs fire off the photons are presumably going to land on opposite sides of the ring - 180 degrees apart. A true understanding of how this decay process results in a picture is complex and well beyond the scope of the exam (trust me, no one writing the questions actually understands it). It will be the same deal when we discuss MRI in the next chapter. In the simplest terms, the computer assumes that these two 511 photons travel in a straight line ( “straight line o f coincidence ”) reality is that the line is never exactly 180 degrees straight. The degree to which the line of response is not linear is referred to as “Non- Collinearity. ” The 511 keV photons will strike the detector elements, which are usually made of some type of scintillation material (related trivia on …

The previous page described the scenario where a pair of photons would be considered to represent a “true coincidence.” This is actually only 1 of 3 possible scenarios where the computer will record an event. There are two additional types of “false coincidences” - which are basically tricks / fuck ups - Scatter and Random. True Coincidence - Two 511 photons resulting from an annihilation reaction are detected in the same coincident window. Scatter Coincidence - One of the photons has a Compton interaction and is deflected, but still hits the detector within the coincident time window (just not in the calculated location). Trivia: The Compton event lowers the photon energy. Therefore you can reduce this by using a more narrow photo-peak window. Random Coincidence: Two photons, from different annihilation reactions just so happen to land within the same coincident window - creating the fa…

Continuing with this discussion.... If the photons meet the selected criteria and a Line Of Response has been generated, that data will be compiled along with all the other LORs to generate a sinogram (buncha wavy lines). That sinogram is then converted into a picture that makes sense to a human via the magic of complex math referred to as “iterative reconstruction algorithms. ” Nuts and Bolts on the Detectors: PET uses Scintillators. Remember that Scintillators are materials that produce light when struck with ionizing radiation (in this case 511 keV photons). The light emitted by the scintillator is then “read” by a photomultiplier. There are several different options for crystal materials and there pros / cons are amendable to multiple choice trivia. THIS vs THAT - SPECT vs PET Crystals: • SPECT: Remember that SPECT uses Nal (activated with a trace of thallium) scintillator crystals. …

Inherent Limitations There are multiple inherent limitation to PET: • Crystal Thickness (number of detectors) - As we discussed on the prior page, the thick crystals you need to use to contain those super high energy 511 photons are going to result in decreased spatial resolution. This is actually the primary limiting factor that determines the spatial resolution of PET. The smaller the crystal used - the more detectors you can have, and the more detectors you have, the better your spatial resolution is. • Positron Range - The positron travels a little bit prior to the annihilation event. The detectors are tracking the position of the event - not the original location of emission. So you are always going to be slightly off. How off you are depends on the energy of the photon. F-l 8 (used for traditional FDG PET) has a maximum range of around 1mm in soft tissue. Rb-82 - used in cardiac PE…

2D vs 3D 2D Systems - These employ lead (or tungsten) septa to block unwanted scatter radiation. The downside to this is that you are also decreasing your sensitivity for un-scattered photons (you excluded the “good guys” too). 3D Systems - These systems do NOT use septa. This results in a large increase in sensitivity, and allows you to decrease the amount of tracer you are giving. Why can you decrease the dose of tracer? Because 2D systems are blocking a ton of activity with those septa (both good and bad). To pull off the 3D imaging you need faster coincidence detector. The classic spots you see 3D done is with CNS imaging (small object - lots of activity) and PEDs (also small object). Potential Disadvantages to 3D • Dead Time - It is possible to overwhelm (and temporarily paralyze) the detectors if you have counts rates that are very high. Another reason a dose decrease is sometimes …

Attenuation Correction Let us begin our discussion on Attenuation Correction by refreshing memories. In the prior pages, we were discussing the various methods the scanner uses to confirm a true event and localize that event along a “line of response.” In that discussion we mentioned that it is possible for scatter to trick the detector, producing an event that is assigned to the incorrect LOR. The additional issue is that when you assign the event to the wrong LOR (wrong spot in the body) you are also removing that event from the correct spot in the body. This removal of events can occur from both Compton interactions and Photoelectric interactions - and be defined with the vocabulary word “Attenuation.” *In PET it is primarily Compton scatter. Attenuation Correction works by assuming that 511 keV photons will be attenuated exponentially by a linear attenuation coefficient (the more tis…

Now having said (written) that, this is really only true if the body is a homogenous attenuator (which it’s not). We are made of different stuff - bones, blood, fat, in some cases silicone, or even metal. In this way it is better to think of PET attenuation as being “Tissue Dependent. ” Before we get into that - let us be clear. I’m not trying to convince you that attenuation does not occur to a greater degree at increased depth - obviously it does. I’m trying to say that having two photons coming off at 180 degrees creates a scenario where things get evened out. This is different compared to the situation for SPECT where you have a single photon at one depth vs another photon at a different depth. This is why PET has a superior capacity to correct for attenuation relative to SPECT. As previously discussed, photons originating from deep structures will undergo greater attenuation relativ…

Gamesmanship: Classic Trick 1: Metal Objects (classic = pacemaker) will generate overcorrected false increased activity at the site. This happens because the density of the metal is outside the conventional range that can be processed. You can see the same thing happen with other very dense objects including: calcified lymph nodes and IV contrast bolus injections (increased activity in the adjacent venous structures). This is why you always double check the uncorrected data when reading a PET. Classic Trick 2: A sneaky trick is to ask you to distinguish between corrected and uncorrected PET. The secret is to look at (1) the skin - which will be hot on uncorrected, and (2) the lungs - which will also be hot on uncorrected. Uncorrected Corrected " Dark Skin - Light Skin ■ Dark Lungs “ Light Lungs 263

Calculation of Standard Uptake Value (SUV): SUVs are not like HU (calibrated to water). There are multiple factors which go into the calculation of SUVs, and essentially make it highly variable from institution-to-institution and even patient-to-patient. The calculation is this: SUV = Tissue Radioactivity Concentration at Time Point 1 x Patient Weight Injected Dose Activity Fat vs Skinny: Fat has a low FDG uptake. So, it’s actually more accurate to use the patients lean body mass instead of weight (this is why the value is different from patient to patient). SUVs in fat people are overestimated (there is more sugar around for the tumor) - this can be corrected for by using lean body mass. Timing: The longer you wait between FDG administration and imaging, the more FDG uptake you get. At first the uptake of FDG in the tumor is rapid (rapid in first 2 hours), then it goes up slower. In oth…

II ■ This has to do with differences in the field of view (FOV) from PET and CT. The classic example is a giant monster fat person who is so fat that they have several feet of blubber outside the CT FOV (and therefore not providing data for attenuation correction). The PET FOV is larger than the CT so it picks up the blubber but the eventual SUV calculation will be fucked because of the mismatch between CT and PET. Truncation A rtifact: < ( ) » Fat Ass in CT Scanner Same Fat Ass Now in PET Scanner -Lesion hanging half out of the FOV -Lesion now seen within FOV -Attenuation artifact / lack there of results in the lesion appearing artificially HOT at the margin, and artificially cold outside of the CT FOV Lesions that arc on the margin of the FOV in the CT scanner may appear hotter than they really are from attenuation over-correction. Lesions that are outside of the CT FOV - but are seen …

Pre FDG PET prep: Diet - Fasting for 4 hours prior to the test is the typical recommendation. If they just ate, they will spike their insulin and drive all the FDG into their muscles. Minimizing cardiac activity (you might want to do if you have a thoracic cancer) can be done with a 12 hour fast, and a low carb/ high protein & fat diet for 24 hours. Hydration - Oral hydration, and frequent voiding decreases the dose to the bladder and improves urinary visualization. M uscle Uptake - There are a couple of causes for diffuse muscle uptake; exercise (most places discourage this for 24-48 hours prior to the exam), eating or insulin use. More focal uptake (classic forearms from stress gripping) can also be seen. Insulin - If you have to have insulin, long acting insulins are better to avoid diffuse muscle uptake. Metformin is ok - it won’t mess with the muscles. Brown Fat - Excessive brown fa…

PET QA Just like there is QA on all the other pieces of equipment in nuclear medicine, PET also has a whole bunch of QA. I’ve chosen two QA tests which I think are the most high yield. I believe they are high yield because they are sorta similar things and that means they’d make good distractors for each other on multiple choice. Blank Scan - DAILY - This can be done two ways. 1. By using a positron source (68Ge or l37Cs) in the scanner all by itself. No phantom. No patient. Just a “blank scan,” irradiating the detector elements uniformly. 2. By using a uniform source - most commonly referred to as a “cylinder.” This cylinder is a 20 cm diameter phantom filled with the 511 keV positron emitting 68Ge/Ga. The “cylinder” is placed at the center of the FOV - and should also irradiate the detector elements uniformly. Don’t be tricked if the question writer refers to a scan that is performed w…

PET QA Continued Normalization Scan - MONTHLY (some sources say at installation, “after major service, when the studies look shitty, or at least annually.”) The scanner is made of a shit ton of detector elements (like 20,000). If you have even the slightest variation is the performance properties (i.e. one crystal is cut slightly thicker than another) you can get small variations in the line of response (LOR) counting. This nonuniform variability can be “normalized” by performing a normalization scan. This is performed similar to a blank scan - either by taking a point source (usually 68Ge) and rotating it around the periphery of the field of view, or using a cylindric source. The idea is to expose the detectors to a uniform flux of positrons. Trivia / Clarification - Regarding 68Ge / 68Ga You will sometimes (often) see 68Ge described as a positron emitter. This is not totally accurate a…
