Disclaim er - Strategy Overview: MRI is a bottomless pit of trivia. It’s very easy to fall into the trap of “trying to know everything” when studying this topic. That is potentially a colossal fuck-up. People literally do PhD work on the stuff and still don’t totally understand it. It boggles the mind how smart the people were who invented this stuff - as it’s very difficult even for “smart people” to get the basic concepts. I could dive into the reasons why “smart” people struggle with learning the topic - cliff notes is I don’t believe most doctors are “smart” - 1 just think they are good at memorizing and repeating stuff. It’s ok - that skill set will work here too. That’s what I’m trying to convey. You do NOT need to master the subject matter. The people writing the questions don’t even understand it that well. You just need basic concepts and that is what I’m going to give you. If y…

W hat is this “Coordinate System ” ? MRI employs a “coordinate system” - to describe which direction the proton(s) are pointing. A point worth making is that when you see an arrow pointing up or down or sideways - this does not represent a single proton, but instead the addition of all the protons as a “vector”. Protons aligned in the direction of the external magnet field are called “longitudinal magnetization.” This is the “default setting” when someone is in the magnet. If they are aligned perpendicular to the Z axis, then they are considered “transverse magnetization.” W hat is this “RF Pulse” ? In my overview I said that you could “slap down” the protons and then time how fast they bounce back up or “recover.” (Using the word “recover” makes you sound smart). The “slapping” is done by a RF pulse. The pulse is a radio wave. What? A “Radio-wave” ? Yup — Remember this thing? “Z” Longit…

So how do you know how “fast to run” , or “w hen to push” ? There is this thing called the Larmor Equation. C0 o y B 0 Written in English: Precession Frequency = Gyromagnetic Ratio x Field Strength in Tesla • CDo is the precession frequency • y is a proportionality constant called the gyromagnetic ratio • Bo is the external magnetic field strength given in Tesla. The Larmor equation describes the precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment and resonant frequency of a nucleus, and relates these aspects to the magnetic field strength. It basically says the precession frequency gets higher as the field strength increases. The kid swings faster. Precession Frequency (COo) is Directly Proportional to Magnet Field Strength (Bo) 272

W hat exactly does the RF pulse do? The RF pulse actually does two things: (1) it decreases the longitudinal magnetization, and (2) it causes the protons to sync up and precess in-phase (which establishes a transverse magnetization). <> VL Z, W hat do you m ean “sync up” ? Before the Pulse Let’s say we are look at this vector diagram from the air - maybe looking down on it from Donald Trump’s Helicopter. What you are seeing is the “transverse plane.” The normal (not-synced) situation is going to be a random rotating / spinning of the protons. Immediately After Pulse Immediately after the fury of the RF pulse - if we looked down on this thing we would see the protons all lined up together. They would be synced up or “in-phase. ” When can you m easure signal? Signal can only be measured when it is NOT in the longitudinal direction. If you are running next to someone at the same speed, you …

W hat is this “ T1 w ? After you knock the protons down with an RF pulse, they will grow back up to normal size (their spin magnitude will re-orient in the direction of Bo). The time it takes for this to happen is different in different tissues. The process is referred to as “longitudinal relaxation”. Some people call this “spin-lattice relaxation ” because energy from the RF pulse is handed over to the surrounding lattice. The “1” of TI resembles a thermometer - which is useful in remembering that TI relaxation involves the exchange of thermal energy. Plotting the time vs longitudinal magnetization creates the TI curve. T1 - Recovery of Longitudinal Magnetization The return to longitudinal relaxation follows an exponential curve approaching 100% over time. TI is defined as the time at which longitudinal magnetization is 63% of its final value. Each tissue has a different TI and the grea…

W hat is this “ T 2 ” ? Remember I said the RJF pulse caused the protons to synch up and precess in phase (which establishes a transverse magnetization). As time progresses the protons will slowly fall out of sync and start doing their own thing again. This is the T2 Transverse relaxation. Time Elapses Plotting the time vs transverse magnetization creates the T2 curve - which resembles the downward portion of a ski slope. I like this analogy because it helps me remember that T2 is shorter than T1 (takes less time to go down a hill than up it). Transverse magnetization decay is also described by an exponential curve with T2 representing the time at which the signal has decayed to 37% of its original value of transverse magnetization (63% of it has decayed). Another term often used to describe T2 is “spin-spin relaxation. ” W hat causes protons to lose th eir transverse sync (T2 relaxation…

T2 vs T2* ? The signal of T2 decays faster than various PhDs would predict based on tissue spin interactions alone. The reason for this is that math falsely assumes the main external field is absolutely homogeneous — it is not. This heterogeneous field creates additional interactions which further speed decay. It is because of this that T2* decay is ALWAYS faster than T2. T2* = Tissue Spin Interaction + Field Inhomogeneity; “random + fixed causes ” • T2 = Tissue Spin Interaction ; “random causes only” 12 Is there a way to “Fix” T2* and make it T2? Yes - but let me define a few things first. TR — Or “Time to Repetition” is the time between the initiation of two successive RF pulses RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse TR 276

“Free Induction Decay” -FID - After you give an RF pulse the protons sync up and you start getting a signal. As time lapses this signal becomes less and less - decay via T2* (“random + fixed causes”) Technically an FID will be created by an RF pulse with any flip angle (90 degree as in spin echo or less than 90 degree as in gradient echo). 1 guess an exception would be a 180 degree pulse as it only inverts the longitudinal magnetization and doesn’t directly generate a transverse component - so pure 180 RF = no FID, but otherwise all Flip Angles cause them. ‘Free Induction Decay’ I thought you w ere going to tell m e how to “fix ” this T 2* thing? Alright this is how it’s done. Remember how we talked about T2 signal “decaying” to a “dephased” state over time? Well if you wait until it’s halfway through the dephasing process and hit it with a 180 degree pulse (the regular RF pulse we’ve be…

W hat is this “Echo” you speak of? If you are in a tunnel and you shout - your voice will trail off and then come back. That’s an echo. Something trailing off and coming back. When you perform your 180 pulse, the signal will build back up and come back. out of phase After 180 peaks in uniformity Pulse - the tip of the echo When do you deliver the 180 pulse? The 180 is given at the 1/2 the “T.E.” - or Time to Echo. TE 278

T1 and T2 - Clarified When people say “T l” or “T2” sequences they are not really telling the whole story. There is no such thing as a pure Tl or pure T2 - they are only “heavily weighted” as one or the other. Let me show you what I mean. Let’s look at the Tl and T2 curves. W hat is this “Proton Density” ? So you have T1 and T2 weighted sequences, and then you have “Proton Density.” So what is this “proton density.” The best way to think about it - that PD is what you have left when you subtract the bias of T1 or T2 weighting. With all things equal the only thing you are measuring is how many protons are present in one thing vs another thing. So how is that accomplished? On the prior page, I showed you that a “short-short” - TR, and TE will result in T1 weighting - by maximizing the longitudinal relaxation differences between tissues and minimizing the transverse relaxation differences. I also showed you that a “long- long” - TR, and TE will result in T2 weighting - by maximizing the transverse relaxation differences between tissues and minimizing the longitudinal differences. Q: So what if you choose a long TR - to minimize longitudinal difference, and cho…

W hat is this k-Space and Im age M atrix ? First let me introduce this thing called a “Fourier Transform.” A fourier transform is a mathematical technique for converting data from the time domain to data in the frequency domain. K-space is a Fourier plane (like an x-y axis coordinate system) in which MR signal is stored. Turning K-space into an image requires an inverse two dimensional Fourier Transform (lots of math... which computers are good at). . ,> . ■. - Vy\ ■» ■ , * r, .. ■ ,• tik-, *- -■ *V*- •• >' y * f J * ? * " V ) ** 1 ; ' - • ' .V V- . -* ' • . S patial Encoding - How Do You Localize the Signal From W ithin the Body ? - Localization of signal requires three steps: (1) Select the desired slice (2) Encode spatial information along the rows (3) Encode spatial information along the columns The localizing gradients have identical properties they are just applied at different times and in different directions. Because you have three gradients in three planes, you can localize anything in the body. (1) Selecting the Desired Slice: The use of a slice selection gradient (SSG) is used to select the area of interest. This gradient is placed perpendicular to the desired slice plane. The slice selection gradient determines the view (axial, coronal, sagittal, even oblique). This vs That Localization Gradients can be turned “on” or “off.” Superconducting “main” magnet is always on. Selective Pulse: On top of this gradient, an RF pulse is appl…

(2) Encoding Spatial Inform ation ~ in Vertical Direction (Phase Encoding): This is the second step in the process. This gradient is applied causing protons in the same row perpendicular to the gradient to have the same phase. All protons at this point will have the same frequency. Phase encoding is much longer than frequency encoding - this is why it’s done on the thinner portion. They don’t want to lose time including the tip of the nose on phase encoding - this is why it’s side to side in the head. The opposite is true in the abdomen, where it’s front to back. Breast is an exception to this rule (I’ll touch on that later). Contribution to duration o f study: The number ofphase encoding steps contributes to the duration o f a 2D imaging sequence. Duration = TRx Npy x Nex TR = Repetition Time NPy = Number o f phase encoding steps Nex = Number o f excitations Phase Encoding Gradient (3) …

Pulling it Together Lets walk through the steps of a basic “spin echo” type of sequence. Spin Echo Sequence ~TR 90 180 RF SSG — PEG ------ FEG Echo TE +— // 90 “ / / ---------------- - / / ----------------- (1) Slice Selection: This SSG is applied at the same time as the 90 degree RF pulse (called a selective pulse). (2) Phase encoding and frequency encoding gradients are applied. (3) Next a 180 refocusing RF pulse is given. Before and after this 180 pulse, two identical SS gradient lobes are applied to help correct undesired spin on the edge of the slice. (4) Lastly a second FEG is applied at the same time as the read out echo. This process is repeated for as many phase encoding steps you do. 284

Slice Thickness For each slice to have a thickness, a “slice” or “band” of nuclei must be excited via the excitation pulse. As I mentioned previously, the slope of this slice selecting gradient will decide the difference in precessional frequency between two adjacent spots on the gradient. In other words, how many phase encoding steps. • NEX = The number of times each s…

OK... Fine! It’s probably true that using a narrower RF pulse bandwidth will take longer to excite, which may result in a slightly longer scan. BUT for the purpose of multiple choice (assuming it’s a 2D standard sequence) I only want you to consider the big 3; TR, Phase Matrix, and NEX. But wouldn’t thinner slices mean more Phase Encoding Steps? Don’t think about it like that. W hat is this “Phase M atrix” ? The is another way of saying “number of phase encoding steps.” You can also think about this as how many lines you need to fill before the scan is done. 1 Line = 1 TR. This is why you multiple #PE steps x TR to calculate scan time. W hat is this “Num ber of Excitations” ? It took me like 3 hours to finish the shading on that upper lip. You put the patient in the scanner (magnet is always on) and a certain number of nuclei align with the field. Not all of them. Some... Lets do this... You have a 0.5 T blur machine because technical fees buy Ferraris, and Ferraris are awesome. At 0.5 T 51% of the nuclei are aligned with the field. So 49% are against it. So the Net is 1%. He has got 80% to work with. More than enough to find that disc bulge (and miss t…

BUT Prometheus! 1 Read that Stronger Magnets Increase the TR! That’s Opposite o f What You Just Said!?! So... let us be clear about some things. There is NOT a knob on the scanner that turns down or up the field strength. It is like ultrasound. If you want a different frequency you need to pick up a different probe. You want 1.5 T instead of 3 T then you need put the patient on a different scanner. BUT you know what there is a knob for? Changing the fucking TE and TR. That’s how you decide how heavily T1 or T2 weight something is or PD or whatever.. Remember the TR controls the amount of recover in the longitudinal direction that is allowed to recover before the next pulse is applied. Long TR: You are giving time for full recovery. Therefore, you are allowing more to be available to be flipped on the next go around. • Short TR: You are NOT allowing for a full recovery. So when the next R…

2: Spatial Resolution Factors affecting spatial resolution: Spatial resolution is governed by the size of a voxel. The voxel size is determined by the matrix, field of view, and slice thickness. € FOV Phase Voxel = Slice Thickness x I X I Matrix Size Phase Field o f View: The smaller the field of view, the better the spatial resolution. So, why not make the FOV super super small? Because you will get aliasing or wrap around artifacts from signal outside the FOV. FOV Read Matrix Size Read Matrix Size: The matrix size corresponds to the image width and height (in pixels). The larger the matrix the smaller the pixels. Pixel = FOV / M atrix Unfortunately, no one can be told what the matrix is, you have to see it for yourself. Gradient: A gradient with higher amplitude (more intense) or one applied for a longer period of time results in better spatial resolution. Makes sense since the point o…

3: Signal to Noise Noise is random variation in the signal that leads to its degradation. In MRI the primary source of this noise is the patient’s body. Overall the SNR depends on voxel size (bigger is better), field strength, and some random sequence parameters. Voxel Size: Anything that makes your voxel bigger improves your SNR (the opposite of spatial resolution). • Thicker slices = Higher SNR • Increased Transmit RF Pulse Bandwidth • Decreased Slice Selection Gradient • Larger FOV = More SNR • Smaller Matrix = More SNR Field Strength: A stronger field gets you more signal. Greater Field Strength = Greater SNR. RF Coils: Smaller surface coils improve your signal (increased SNR) compared to a coil within the scanner. Number o f Excitations per Slice (number o f averages): The more excitations you perform the more signal you get (increased SNR). The main trade off is a prolonged imaging…

Potential Fuckery - Receiver Bandwidth vs Transm it Bandwidth Don’t confuse these terms - they sound similar and would make good distractors. Fat Receiver Bandwidth = Decrease SNR, Fat Transmit Bandwidth = Large Slice = Increased SNR Narrow Receiver Bandwidth = Increased SNR. Narrow Transmit Bandwidth = Thin Slice = Decreased SNR Proton Density - Signal to Noise Specialist If you ask a MSK guy the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “I like it for the meniscus.” If you ask a Neuro guy the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “I like it for MS Plaques in the Posterior Fossa.” If you ask a physicist the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “it has a good signal to noise ratio.” All of them are correct - for the same reason. MRI Sequences There are a bunch of sequences, but two general categories: Spin Echo - which uses a 90 degree flip angle and a 180 degree refocusing RF pulse Gradient Echo - which uses a LESS than 90 degree flip angle, *and NO 180 RF pulse Spin Echo: Spin echo is composed of a 90 RF pulse followed by an 180 rephasing pulse. This rephasing pulse is done at 1/2 the time of echo Spin Echo Sequence JR — ----- 180 rephasing pulse is done at 1/2 the time of echo A (TE). The sequence is repeated (TR) until k ^ A r \j \J ' space is filled. ggQ ■ ________ j f - FEG Why do you give this 180 pulse? In an ideal P E G €=§5 f j ~ world the magnetic field is homogeneous, but we don’t live in an ideal world. The pulse is given to try and improve the heterogeneous / nature of the field and this is the reason spin |— echoes give us T2 and not T2*. As discussed in the spatial encoding section, the slice sel…

Fast Spin Echo Fast Spin Echo The idea behind the FSE sequence is to reduce the TR, which I already mentioned is a major contribution to the duration of the study. This is done by applying multiple 180 RF pulses each resulting in an echo. Echo Train Length - Number of echoes in the same TR. Fat Signal - There is a “normal” phenomenon called “J coupling” which occurs between the nuclei of lipid molecules, causing intrinsic shortening of T2 signal. The fast repetition of 180 degree pulses mess up the J couples and cause the T2 of fat to lengthen. Testable Point: T2 fat signal is longer with fast spin echo (interferes with J coupling). Testable Vocab: With each progressive echo train the transverse signal gradually decreases. This is called “T2 Blurring” Testable Point: Acquisition time is approximately proportional to 1/ETL 298

Inversion Recovery Inversion Recovery Sequence -------------------------- TR --------------- TI 1 RF SSG 180 A 90 180 A IJO m V \J U iT m PEG FEG i Echo ------4-, 180 ■; / i A r -II- Tl 100-180 msec 180 r h ”. 180 \j 180 I I f TI 1700-2200 msec n r IT 180 Jl This time instead of starting with a 90 degree RF pulse, you start with a 180 degree “preparation” pulse. You wait for the relaxation of the thing you want to saturate (water, fat, myocardium) to hit its null point then you slam it with the 90 degree pulse. This way that particular tissue gives no signal. “TI” - The time between the 180 and 90 pulse. “STIR ” - Short TI Inversion Recovery, employs a short TI (120- 160 ms) to suppress fat, which is based on the TI for fat. Often used on MSK imaging to suppress the fat within bone marrow to allow visualization of fluid (edema) within bone. “FLAIR ” - Fluid Attenuated Inversion Recovery,…

GRE Sequences Gradient echo sequences are different than spin echo sequences because they (1) use a flip angle less than 90 and (2) do NOT have a 180 pulse. The advantage to this low flip angle is faster recovery, shorter TR/TE times, and a faster scan. The flip angle is used to determine how much transverse and how much longitudinal magnetization is going to be used. Testable Point: Because there is no 180 pulse you are dealing with T2* (not T2). For the same reason GRE is more susceptible to susceptibility artifacts. Testable Point: Gradient sequences have a lower Specific Absorption Rate (less heating). I f there is no 180 pulse, how do you create an echo? A bipolar readout gradient (basically a frequency encoding gradient) is used. Vocab: An echo in gradient is called a “Field Echo” I f gradient is so fast and has a nice low SAR, why don’t we use it fo r everything? The signal to noi…

Echo-Plannar Im aging (EPI) “T h e Noisy O ne” 90 180 RF v l r SSG PEG FEG Echo — . . •> ' — [.....I..... i......n r z T f 1 » a i 3 1 E r I 1 I E6 67 TE EPI can be done with spin echo (90 + 180) or gradient echo (90 + a bunch of gradients). EPI is MRI’s fastest acquisition method, on the order of 100 ms/slice. One RF pulse is used to acquire data for an image (aka single shot). The sequence works by turning the phase encoding and frequency encoding gradients on and off very rapidly - causing a very fast filling of k space. Compared to a regular GRE: The sequence is more vulnerable to magnetic susceptibility, gives you better tissue contrast, and is faster. Artifacts linked to EPI: Magnetic Susceptibility - can be improved with segmented sequences (instead of single shots) Ghosting - Gradient imperfections mess with spatial encoding Chemical Shift - A narrow readout bandwidth is used. As…

Diffusion The base sequence is either a fast GRE or an echo planar. Generally speaking, diffusion weighted imaging works by using two very strong and symmetric MR gradients. The acquisition is repeated in each of the 3 dimensions in space with b-factors of 0 and b-factors of 1000. The signal differences are based on mobility and direction of water. Scenario 1 (no net movement): The first gradient fires dephasing the spins. The molecules do not move. The second gradient fires rephasing the same molecules - giving you high signal. Scenario 2 (net movement): The first gradient fires dephasing the spins. The molecules move out of the way. The second gradient fires missing the original protons - gives you low signal. Vocab: The direction of diffusion is described as isotropic (movement in all spatial directions) and anisotropic (movement in a single direction). “B-Factor” - The best way to th…

Additional Sequences - Mentioned For Completeness: Perfusion imaging • Perfusion MRI quantitates cerebral micro-vascularization parameters. These parameters include regional blood flow and blood volume and mean transit time. Functional MRI (fMRI) • The basics underlying fMRI are increased blood flow to local vasculature that accompanies neural activity resulting in local reduction of deoxyhemoglobin. Deoxyhemoglobin acts as a contrast agent because it is paramagnetic (thus alters T2* MR signal). Take home point = fMRI depends on T2* effects. In fMRI procedures: First the patient performs a task, then Blood Oxygen Level Dependent (BOLD) imaging is done before and after the task. The pre and post task images are then subtracted and overlaid on a gray-scale brain image to localize the signal origin. Angiography: 2D time o f flight (TOF) MRA, 3D-TOF MRA, Phase contrast MRA • 2D-TOFMRA: Uses …

MRI Spectroscopy WTF is this “M R I Spectroscopy ” ? The general idea is that the various metabolites that exist on the cellular level (choline, lactate, N-acetylaspartate “NAA,” etc... etc...) occur in different concentrations depending on the pathology. For example, “NAA” is a neuronal marker. Things that destroy neurons (like tumors) will decrease NAA. So, in general the lower the NAA the higher the grade tumor. I talk about this stuff in volume 2 o f Crack the Core. How is this sorcery performed ? The physics is pretty similar to conventional MRI. In fact, you can used a variety o f sequence types including vary basic spin ECHO. Some difference worth knowing are: (1) Instead o f using the information from the frequency gradient to provide locational information - that data is used to help identify the different chemical patterns in a methods similar to the way NMR worked in your unde…

Fat Saturation Techniques There are two broad categories. •Inversion Sequences (STIR) that are based on the inversion time “T.I.” of fat •Those that exploit the resonance difference between fat and water protons. STIR - As described previously, this is a short inversion sequence (180, followed by a well timed 90). It does great with metal artifact. You can NOT use Gd with it. Selective Pulse: Remember one of the limitation of STIR is that you can’t give Gd with it - so this is the method used most commonly for contrast enhanced studies. Water Unlike STIR which works by capitalizing on the differences in inversion times. Selective saturation works by capitalizing on the resonance difference between protons within their respective microenvironment. For example, protons in fat precess about 74 Hz slower than water at 0.5 Hz. This precession difference gets more exaggerated with a stronger m…

Fat Related Topics: In Phase and Out of Phase: The chemical environments of fat and water are different for protons. This causes these protons to precess at different rates. To create “in and out of phase” images a spoiled GRE is performed when the protons are spinning with each other (about 4.4 msec at 1.5T) and directly out of phase of each other (2.2 msec at 1.5 T). Think about this like two runners on a track. At the beginning of the race they are lined up together. The faster of the two (water) will get out to a lead - and at 2.2msec (assuming a 1,5T magnet) will be directly across the track from the slow one (fat) - this is when they are “opposed.” At this point in time their signal will cancel out. As the race continues eventually the fast one (water) will begin to lap the slow one (fat) - and at 4.4msec will have gone all the way around and cut him. At this point they are in phas…

Does it matter when (chronologically) you obtain the out of phase sequence? In other words in you obtain out of phase at 2.2 msec, and in phase at 4.4 msec is that the same as getting out of phase at 6.6 msec and in phase at 4.4 msec ? Yes/No - But ultimately yes. The No - If you are looking for Hepatic Steatosis, then it does NOT matter. If the liver is fatty you will see signal loss on out of phase no matter when you get it. Out of phase is out of phase, and the reason out of phase is out of phase is because of the precision differences in fat and water. The water is lapping the fat on the track. The Yes - What about Hemochromatosis? The iron liver. Remember way back to the beginning of this masterpiece of a chapter when I talked about T2*? The quick refresher is basically loss of signal occurs faster when you have an inhomogeneous field. The 180 degree pulse of Spin Echo is used to tr…

Chemical Shift Artifact (The Fat Artifact): This will be mentioned again when 1 talk about artifacts, but I want to just touch on it briefly as it’s related to fat. Again, because of the difference in proton environments between fat and water precession differences cause an artifact in the read out (frequency encoding) direction. There are two subtypes. Type 1: This one manifestation is a bright rim on one side and a dark rim on the other. This can occur on either Spin Echo or GE sequences. Testable Trivia: Chemical shift increases with field strength (it’s not seen below 1 T) Testable Trivia: Chemical shift decreases with increased gradient strength Testable Trivia: Chemical shift decreases with a wider read out bandwidth Type 1 Chemical Shift Artifact Frequency Encoding Direction Type 2: India Ink Artifact (Black Boundary) - This type of chemical shift artifact shows a black line in al…

MR Contrast (Gd+) Broadly speaking MRI contrast can be grouped into either “positive agents” which cause bright Tl signal (shortens Tl), or “negative agents” which produce magnetic inhomogeneity from susceptibility leading the T2 shortening. Gadolinium: This is a highly toxic metal that can cause all kinds of badness even in small doses - including liver necrosis. To solve this issue, Gd is complexed with a chelate. The toxicity, clearance routes, bio-distribution, and relaxation properties are all attributes to the chelating agent (DTPA). How do Gd+3 cause a Tl shortening? • Short answer: Gd is strongly paramagnetic. “Paramagnetic” means it can be magnetized, and when it does, it tends to be polarized in a way that is the same as the magnetic field - locally augmenting it. • Longer answer: Gd+3has seven unpaired electrons, and the interaction of these ELECTRONS causes augmentation of th…

Yes... you probably need to know both names because the question writer could be an asshole and give you the name no one uses. MRI Artifacts A lot of artifact testable fodder has to do with phase vs frequency encoding direction. So let us review that briefly, then we will discuss the artifacts. When we sample signal for each plane we have an “X” and a “Y” direction for the grid - one will be phase the other frequency. You CAN do it either way, and sometimes flipping them is a way to deal with flow (motion) or wrap around (aliasing) artifacts. What influences the choice? A - Scan Time. Remember that the number of phase encoding steps is a key component to the table time equation. More steps = More time. That is why scan time is the most important consideration, because scan time = money (and despite what Academic Radiologists will tell you: money = happiness). The Hospital Administrator who went to school for 6 years and makes triple what your Chairman makes would agree also. As a general rule phase encoding dire…

Image Process Artifacts: These include aliasing, chemical shift, truncation, and partial volume. Aliasing: This error occurs when an area is under sampled. You get wrapping of anatomy from under sampled portions. This occurs in the phase encoding direction. Fix: • MAKE THE FOY BIGGER: Seems obvious. The follow-up question is this... what is the cost? Why not just make the FOVfucking huge every time to avoid this? The answer is spatial resolution. Large FOV = Shitty Spatial Resolution. Ever try and look at the spine on a body MRI? Think you can grade narrowing/stenosis accurately on that mother fucker? The reason it is blurry is because your FOV is large. Large FOV = Shitty Spatial Resolution. So the counter to the counter is that you have to increase phase encoding steps to maintain the spatial resolution -which will lengthen the time of the scan, cost the imaging center money, and get y…

Chemical Shift: As discussed (page 291), the protons of different molecules precess at different frequencies. The difference between water and fat is around 220 Hz at 1.5 T (even worse at 3 T). This shift in the Larmor frequency is the so called “chemical shift.” The type 1 error occurs in the frequency encoding direction (opposite of most of artifacts which are P.E.). Fixing Type 1: • Usually this doesn’t cause a big enough problem to need “fixing” but for the purpose of multiple choice exams here are a few solutions. • Swap PE and FE positions to move the artifact off the area of interest. • Use STIR or other fat suppression techniques • Increase the Receiver Bandwidth Fixing Type 2: • Adjust the TE • Perform Spin Echo Sequence (remember this only occurs with GRE) Partial Volume: This is just like in CT. You have different signal intensities in different adjacent structures. If they ov…

Truncation I Gibbs: The transformation of K space through inverse fourier transform ideally (but never) resulting in a block of data. Ripples in this data - especially at abrupt intense tissue changes result in the appearance of lines. You classically see these at high contrast interfaces (skull -brain, Cord-CSF, and meniscus/fluid). The CSF-Cord interface is the most classic - mimicking a syrinx. If prompted I would say the cause is limited sampling of free induction decay. It can be seen in both the frequency encoding and phase encoding directions but is more commonly seen in the phase encoding because many times a phase encoding matrix that is smaller than the readout matrix is selected to reduce time. Gamesmanship: The classic way to show this is sagittal view of the spine - looking like a syrinx. Making it better: • Short answer = more matrix. • Long answer = Decreasing the bandwidt…

Motion: This can be seen with both voluntary and involuntary (cardiac, respiratory) movements. Motion creates difference between frequency encoding (which is fast) and phase encoding (which is slow). You will see ghosting or smearing - primarily in the phase encoding direction. You can also see the classic pulsation artifact from the aorta. Making it Better: • If the motion is “voluntary” you should start by yelling at the patient (“SIR! SIR! PLEASE STOP MASTURBATING! - You are creating a motion artifact that is degrading imaging quality!). • Perform Breath Holding - This is the best method for respiratory motion. • Respiratory Gating (increases acquisition time) • ROPE (Respiratory Ordered Phase Encoding) - Phase encoding steps are ordered with respiration • Breathing Navigator - An echo from the diagram determines its position, then timing and acquisition are based off this. • Apply a …

Flow: This is a type of motion artifact, related to blood flow. Blood flow causes ghosting in the phase encoding direction. GRE sequences are more susceptible to this than SE sequences. SE Sequences - Flow looks dark. Moving blood that got hit with the 90 pulse, moves out of the way prior to getting the 180 pulse. Therefore, it doesn’t have any signal. GRE Sequences: In flowing blood looks bright. Making it better: • Apply a saturation band adjacent to the imaging section - 90 pulse followed by crusher gradient. W hat M akes A Flow Void? The dark hole of a patent vessel is called a “flow void.” This is generally a spin echo imaging finding (as stated above - flow looks dark on SE). ■ 90 180 90+180 1 ■._ No Echo ♦ 11 No Flow, gets 90 & 180- makes an Echo Slow Flow, gets some 90 & 180 - makes an Echo Fast Flow, gets 90 but misses 180 - No Echo Why does this happen? To make an echo, the pro…

Magic Angle: This is an MSK artifact seen with tendons. You see this with short echo time (TE) sequences where the focus forms an angle of 55 degrees with the main magnetic field (magic angle phenomenon). This will NOT be seen in T2 sequences (with long TE). This phenomenon, is reduced at higher field strengths due to greater shortening of T2 relaxation times. Magic Angle: You see it on Short TE (T1, PD, GRE) Then it goes away on Long TE (T2) Why does it go away with a long TE ? Remember “T2” is 37% of the original signal. Tendons that interact with the magnet have a decrease in tissue interactions. The result is a prolonged time needed to get to that 37%. However, if you wait long enough (longer TEs) those differences equal out. This is why longer TE sequences (classic “T2”) are not troubled by the mischief of this sinister phenomenon. 37% Magic Angle Voodoo ■ increases tendon 1 Normal …

Cross Talk: RF and FT pulses are not perfectly rectangular. So if they are placed close enough together then you get excitation of neighboring section more than once in a single repetition. These lead to partial saturation and lower signal. So all sections (except the ones on the ends will be subjected to this). RF Related: These include Cross Talk and Zipper Artifacts Making it better: • Increasing the gap between sections. • Interleave slices (all odds, then all evens). Testable Trivia: The 3D images are not susceptible to this artifact because the entire volume undergoes excitation with sections within the volume acquired with gradients. Zipper A rtifact: There are lots of random stray RF signals (radio, tv, etc...). Remember that the RF pulse is a “radio-wave.” Anyway, if you have defective or inadequate shielding you can get a “zipper” of high signal - 1-2 pixels in width running ac…

External Field Related: This is Magnetic Inhomogeneity: Inhomogeneous Fat Suppression: If the field is actually homogeneous you get a uniform fat saturation by applying an RF pulse with the resonance of fat protons. In the real world, local field inhomogeneities cause fat protons to precess at difference frequencies. These differences allow certain areas of fat to resist suppression - which can mimic edema. Making it better: Using an inversion recovery - STIR, especially in the setting of metal. T2FS: Fat Sat in Marrow is ruined by Improved with STIR Metal messing with the local field Shimming Shimming is a thing that can be done to improve field homogeneity.When the magnet comes from the manufacturer the field homogeneity is terrible. To correct for this a process called “shimming” is used. There are two types of shimming, passive and active. Most of the time a combination of the two is…

Magnetic Susceptibility: This includes diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic Susceptibility (includes “m etal”) - This refers to the ability of a substance to become magnetized by the external field. Obviously metals have large susceptibility. Calcium hydroxy apatite and accumulations of gadolinium chelate can do the same thing. Generally speaking, susceptibility affects all pulse sequences, but is most severe with GRE images and least severe with SE (because of the 180 degree refocusing pulse - to lose those T2* effects). A less prominent version occurs at tissue interfaces (bone and muscle, or air and bone). The classic location for this is the transition from paranasal sinus to skull base. Making it better: • Using SE and FSE instead of GRE. • Reduce the field strength (move them off the 3T onto the 1.5) • Fast / Turbo Spin Echo sequences - High receiver bandwidth and short ech…

Gradient Related: This includes Eddy Currents Eddy Current: These things called “Eddy Currents” are generated when gradients are rapidly turned on and off. The actual location of said currents can be in the magnet, the cables, the wires or even in the patient. This looks like distortion (contraction or dilation of the image) or shift/shear. Testable Trivia: Most severe with DW1 pulses. Making it better: Optimizing the sequence of gradient pulses. FLAIR - for non distorted Diffusion - Notice the stretch / smear at the brain - bone comparison interface. This is from Eddy Currents. 321

Errors in Data: This includes dielectric effects and crisscross Dielectric Effects / Standing Wave effects - Biologic tissues have a dielectric constant that results in reduction of wavelength by the inverse of some constant. Interactions can cause local eddy currents in the imaged tissues. Since RF waves are shorter at 3T - the effects are worse with a stronger magnet. You also see this worsen with large bellies, especially if they have ascites. Larger body parts (the abdomen) are primarily affected. Classic Look / Location: Dark signal in the central abdomen over the left lobe of the liver. Making it better: •Application of dielectric pads - placed between patient and anterior body array coil •Parallel RF transmission (SENSE) - RF pulses from a set of coils; each coil sends an independent RF pulse. Gives you a longer pulse. Crisscross or Herringbone: If you see obliquely oriented strip…

Special Topic - Cardiac MRI It’s best to think of cardiac imaging in two flavors: (1) Bright Blood, and (2) Dark Blood. Bright Blood: These are gradient sequences, and they make up the majority of the cardiac work load. Steady State Free Precession “SSFP” - This is a type of gradient sequence that is the primary sequence used for wall motion and volume analysis. If you see a CINE MRI of the heart it is probably a SSFP. Dark Blood: The short answer is spin echo sequence. The longer answer is double inversion recovery. WTF is “double inversion ” ? Basically it’s two consecutive 180 pulses - one that is “non- selective” and the next which is “selective” to the blood and heart. Basically what you end up with is that all your in-slice tissue is left alone, and your blood (which is flowing into the slice) is nulled - hence the black blood. Why use black blood? It’s good for anatomy. Also becau…

Special Topic - Breast MRI There are a few artifacts and technical differences specific to breast MRI that are worth knowing about. MRI of the breast is done for several reasons (high risk screening, implant rupture evaluation, surgical planning, etc...). The reason for the exam will dictate sequences. Testable Trivia: Breast CA screening will have dynamic post contrast sequences Testable Trivia: Implant Rupture screening will NOT have contrast, but instead have a fat and water saturated sequence (only silicone will be bright). Testable Trivia: A breast specific coil is used to increase SNR. Artifacts: Chemical Shift: You see this all the time in breast MRI, at the fat water interface. Remember this can be corrected by increasing the bandwidth to capture both fat and water in the same phase. Motion / Breathing / Cardiac: Breathing and the beating of the heart would normally degrade the i…

Safety, B ioeffects, and FDA lim its I want you to think about MRI safety in two basic categories: (1) Potential Safety Risk of the Magnet Systems (of which there are 3 categories) (2) Patient Exposure Limits (set by the FDA). Potential Safety Risks - There are 3 main categories broken down by the primary systems involved. (1) Main Magnet, (2) Gradient Set, and (3) RF Transmit-Receive. - Main M agnet - Quick overview into how the magnet works: The magnet is basically a large coil of wire, wrapped around the bore of the magnet. When you apply an electric current through the wire you generate a magnetic field. Obviously the magnet is a very strong magnet so it requires a shit ton of juice (100s of Amps). To put that much juice through a coil of wires requires you to maintain a very low temperature (helps reduce resistance). MR scanner keep the wires cold by submerging the coil (within a ca…

Emergency Shutdown - Cont Real life advice - Always have someone else push the button - preferably a tech, medical student, or non-English speaking observer. It costs the hospital a TON of money - and they will want your balls if you touch that button. Have the med student do it - tell him it’s required for the rotation. Then when hospital administration comes after you - do what your attending would do to you - deny you ever told him to do it. No one will believe him - especially when he’s wearing one of those dorky short white coats. Quench - If the hospital administrators don’t kill you, the actual quench might. The process alone can be dangerous. Remember it works by venting all that liquid helium out of the room (and building) through a pipe. Well.... what if there is a hole in the pipe? Have you seen the maintenance workers at your hospital? Those are the guys putting the pipe in. …

Implanted Devices: Speaking of implanted devices, now is as good of a time as any to discuss the trivia related to these things. There are book (large books) full of safe vs unsafe devices. Compatibility is classified as either - Safe, Unsafe, or Conditional. Conditional means safe for specific MR environments - for example a certain magnet strength, etc... Each of these classifications comes with a specific sign. MR Conditional MR Unsafe MR Safe There are so many damn devices that are safe or unsafe that it is simply not worth the effort to try and memorize them all. Plus, the list is constantly in flux as manufacturers create new “MR Safe” versions of devices. Am I saying the question writers will not ask you about a device ? Of course not. There is no limit to the bullshit they might ask. 1 am saying it is extremely low yield and not worth the effort. If the different between passing …

- G radient Set- Noise: MRIs are noisy, and they can be very noisy depending on the sequences you run. The noise (most of it anyway) actually originates from the gradient coils - secondary to rapid changes in current. Testable Trivia: Gradient Intensive Sequences - such as echo-planar imaging are the loudest. Remember diffusion is an example of echo-planar. Testable Trivia: The FDA sets a maximum of 140 dB for MR (99dB for patients with hearing protection). Testable Trivia: Damage to the fetal ossicles is part of the theoretical (complete bullshit) reason fetal MRI / pregnancy MRI is considered possibly dangerous. The real reason is Radiologists tend to be dickless weenies (present company excluded of course). Neurostimulation: Not as fun as it sounds... What we are talking about here are induced electrical currents leading to painful neurostimulation. You typically hear people complain …

- RF Transm it - Receive - SAR - This brings us to a discussion on SAR. SAR stands for Specific Absorption Rate - which is basically describes the ability to cook (heat) a patient via the energy of the RF pulse. The scientific quantification of this is measured in watts/kg Let’s look at the formula - only for understanding some possible questions. SAR = Bo2 x Alpha2 x Duty Cycle Let me explain it - because it’s written in “math talk.” Bo2 The strength of the magnet. The Important Relationship is the “2.” So if you double the field strength from 1.5T to 3.0T then you quadrupled your SAR. Alpha2 Alpha is the “Flip Angle.” Again the Important Relationship is the “2.” So if you double the flip angle from 15 to 30 then you doubled your SAR. You can infer that spin echo - with a 90 degree flip is going to have more SAR than a GRE with flips less than 90. Duty Cycle - 1 like to think about this…

Misc Heating / Burning Issues: " Touching The Bore - Nonconducting pads are used to provide separation between the skin and the side of the magnet bore, which usually contains both radiofrequency and gradient coils. Touching the bore = possible burn. Way to prevent? Pad the skin. " Cables, Jewelry, and even Medicated Patches can heat up and bum the patient. Remove them prior to the scan. ■ Douchey silvery shirts - i.e.“Affliction” shirts , shouldn’t be worn into the magnet - they can heat up and bum. Hospital gown is the safe thing to put the patient in. ■ Closed Loops - It’s possible that a bum causing loop can be formed between one extremity touching another (hand on leg, leg on leg). The solution is to pad the area between the knees. " “Antenna Effect” - occurs when an electric field resonant coupling with a wire. When can that happen? Abandoned Intracardiac Pacemaker Lead - are at ri…

MRI Zones What are the 4 zones? Zone I: No restriction. This is basically outside the building. Zone II: No restriction. This is the waiting room and the dressing room. This is where you can screen patients and control access to Zone 3 and 4. Zone III: Restricted Room. This is typically the control room, where the MRI tech does his/her thing. There should be some kind of a lock on the door between zone 2 and 3. Zone IV: Restricted Room. This is the actual MRI scanner room (the same room as the magnet). Zone 1: Outside the Building Zone 2: Dressing Room Zone 2: Reception/ Waiting Room Zone 3: Control Room Zone 4i Magnet f 'O * i V ^ i '**0 1s 332

MRI Zone Trivia Are there any exceptions for entry into restricted access zones? Access is restricted to zone 3 and 4 (without filling out the screening forms ect.. There are NO exceptions to the guidelines restricting entry to zone 3 and 4. Even if the patient codes in the scanner there are no exceptions (per the Kanal article cited below). The MRI techs should get them out of the scanner, start CPR / stabilize the patient then get them out — preferably into zone 2 for the code team. Depending on who you ask or what you read - it might be ok to code them in zone 3 (never zone 4). The cleanest answer would be code them in zone 2. If there is a code in the scanner should you quench the magnet? No!. The magnetic field will still be around for a minute or two. To start out with: KERMA stands for “kinetic energy released per unit mass.” I know that’s not at all helpful for understanding it - but I just want you to realize it stands for something. The concept is this - KERMA is a measurement someone invented to help estimate how much PRIMARY energy actually gets transferred when you expose a person to x-rays (or any type of radiation). Why did I write PRIMARY in caps and underline it? Well because I want you to imagine that I yelled that word at you. So WTF do I mean by “PRIMARY” ? What is “Kerma-Area Product (KAP)” also called “Dose Area Product” ?- This is the amount of Kerma (potential dose) multiplied by the cross sectional area of the x-ray beam. This provides a better way to measure the total radiation potentially incident on the patient. Think of it more as a measurement of total radiation used in the exam, more than the actual dose in the patient. KAP = Dose x Cross Sectional Area Conceptually understand that if you increase the juice, but concentrated it in a smaller area, that you might not change your KAP (even though you increased the juice). In teractio n of R adiation w ith Tissue As discussed before, you have two primary methods with which x-rays interact with matter; the photoelectric effect and compton scatter. The key interaction is the production of a free electron, which can then transfer its energy to other electrons by ionization and excitation. The actual damage done is the result of ionization produced by x-rays / gamma ray photons giving energy to orbital electrons and alpha / beta particles interacting electromagnetically with orbital electrons. What Determines the Biologic Effects from Ionizing Radiation? Primary variables include those inherent to the cells and the conditions of the cells at the time of irradiation. There are also variables related to the radiation (absorbed dose, dose rate, type of radiation, and energy of radiation). Damage to biologic systems occurs in this order: Molecular -> Cellular -> O…

Charged Particle Tracks: When radiation crashes into a biologic material, energy is deposited along the tract. The pattern of this deposited energy depends on the type of radiation involved. X-Rays / Gamma Rays: X-ray ionization density is low along the track Neutrons, Protons, and Alpha Particles: Ionization events occur much more frequently. Electron Scattering: tends to have a length greater than its range / depth. Heavy Particles: tend to have a linear path that is equal to its range Concepts / Vocabulary: Linear Energy Transfer (LET) - This is the average amount of energy deposited per unit path length of the incident radiation. LET is important for assessing the potential tissue and organ damage. LET comes in two flavors: • High LET: Neutrons, Protons, Alpha Particles, and Heavy Ions. These are MUCH MORE DAMAGING (“has a higher quality factor”). • Low LET: Photons, Gamma Rays, Elec…

The “Kill Effect* As LET increases, RBE will increase... to a certain point. Above 100 keV/micrometer of tissue, RBE decreases with increasing LET - because the maximum potential damage has already been done - additional increase in LET is wasted dose. Oxygen Enhancement Ratio (OER) - This is the relative effectiveness of radiation to produce damage at different oxygen levels. The idea is that biologic tissue is more sensitive to radiation in an oxygenated state. A testable piece of trivia is that OER really only matters for low LET radiation. With high LET radiation the OER is often 1 (biologic damage without oxygen = biologic damage with oxygen). Direct vs Indirect Ionizing Radiation: Actions are called “direct” if they act “directly on the DNA.” Actions are called “indirect” if they act on water. The majority of irradiation in living cells is the result of indirect action on water (yo…

Effects on DNA Single Strand Break - Ionizing radiation can cause a break in one of the chemical bonds (point mutation). This is more likely with low - LET radiation. Repair enzymes can move in and fix this. Double Strand Break - Ionizing radiation can cause a break in multiple chemical bonds. These are more likely with high - LET radiation. These are harder to fix. Mutation - It’s possible for radiation to cause a loss of or change in the nitrogenous base in the DNA chain. If the cell doesn’t die from this, this incorrect information will be transferred as the cell divides. Testable Point: The syndrome with the most sensitivity to x-rays is Ataxia Telangiectasia. Common distractors include Bloom Syndrome and Fanconi Anemia (both of which have genetic instability but no particular relationship with x-rays). Xeroderma pigmentosa is another classic distractor - but it is more sensitive to …

Effect on the Cell: Testable Point - An x-ray or gamma ray dose of 1000 Gy in a period of second / minutes will cause instant death of a large number of cells. “Mitotic Death ” - This is when a cell dies after 1 or more divisions. A relatively small dose of radiation can cause this. “Mitotic Delay ” -A very small dose (0.01 Gy) just before a cell starts to divide can cause a delay or failure in the timing of the normal dividing. Cell Cycle Phase - Cells are MOST sensitive during M phase (mitosis). They are more sensitive during late G2 phase. They are less sensitive during G1 phase. There are the LEAST sensitive during late S phase. Testable Point: In order of sensitivity: M > G2 > G1 > S. Testable Point: G1 is the part of the cell cycle that is the most variable in length (shorter in cells that turn over quickly). Surviving Cell Synchronization - If you blast a group of cells with radia…

The Survival Curve (possible source of questions). This is a graph of dose vs cell survival. The point to notice is the “Quasi-threshold dose” which is the portion of the graph when repair mechanisms are in there trying to hold the ship together. It is a measure of “sub-lethal” damage to the cell. A testable point is that this shoulder only exists with low-LET radiation curves. Dose 1 / Slope (Do) describes the linear portion of the curve, and the radio-sensitivity of the cell population. The higher the Do the more radio-resistant the cell is. Key Points: Higher Dose Rate makes a smaller shoulder, and steeper drop in the curve • Low LET will have a shoulder, High LET will NOT have a shoulder • Oxygen will make a steeper drop in the curve (more pronounced with low LET) Effect of Ionizing R adiation of Blood: Lymphocytes are the MOST sensitive blood cells in the body. A dose of 0.25 Gy is …

You start out feeling terrible (GI flu like symptoms - nausea / vomiting / diarrhea), then you feel better (latent phase), then your syndrome subtype manifests - related to underlying organ system as below, the fourth phase is death/recovery. But then a song comes on the radio that you really like and you forget to put the car back in drive - so you are sitting there waiting for the light to turn green with the car in reverse. You are truly an accident waiting to happen. This is a latent error. P areto C harts This is a chart named after Vifredo Pareto, who accomplished many great things in his life including popularizing the term “elite” , marrying a women 30 years his junior (WIN!), and collecting a large number of cats - which he famously treated better than his house guests (he fed the cats first, and made his dinner guests wait). You take causes to a problem, and rank them in the order that they contribute to that problem. The problems sum together to 100% of the problem (bars are ranked in descending order, creating a concave function). The point is to show how 2 or 3 things can cause 80% of the issue. R eceiver O perator Curve - ROC This is a graph to describe how good your test is. The perfect (accurate) test will have an area under the curve of 1.0. The worst piece of shit test will have an area under the curve of 0.5 (50% chance, i.e no better than a coin flip) The graph shows the tradeoff between sensitivity and specificity. Questions You Can Ask About ROC Curves: (1)What are the axes of the graph ? Sensitivity, and 1-Specificity (2) What kind of a line would have no predictive value ? The straight one as above (3) What kind of a line would occur from random chance? Same question, just different wording - still the straight one as above. (4) Where is the “Ideal” or “Perfect” or “Gold Standard” test? Top left comer. (5) When the accuracy improves how does the curve change? Shifts towards the upper left comer. 398

There is just no escape from this crap. It has been on every major certifying / board exam you’ve taken, and will be on every one to follow. The Normal Distribution: This will look familiar as it is how every “good test” will turn out. Naturally occurring phenomena will distribute as a bell-shaped “normal” or “Gaussian Distribution.” Data can be “skewed” to one side or the other. The “negative” or “positive” skewing is described by which side the tail is on. The relationship to the mean is the most likely testable trivia. A negative skew has the mean after the tail. A positive skew has the mean before the tail. 413

Precision and A ccuracy: Precision - This is the immunity to variation. “The dart hits the same spot every time.” A wider confidence interval = a less precise study. Accuracy (unbiased) - This is the immunity to systematic error or bias. “The dart hits the center.” W hat is this “S ta tis tica l S ignificance” ? You often hear people say “the study was significant, with a p value < 0.05.” What the hell does that mean anyway? Basically, they are saying that the result is unlikely to have occurred by chance. You can flip a coin that lands on heads 50 times in a row, it’s just not very likely to occur by chance. If you say the p value is < 0.05, then you are saying the likelihood it occurred by chance is less than 5%. In other words, the investigator is 95% sure the result did NOT occur by chance. Errors People will talk about error as either “type 1” or “type 2.” Type 1 Error: The is a false positive. You called the CXR back for a “nodule” but it turned out to be a peanut caught in one of the patient’s many fat folds. Alternatively you could think about this as a fire alarm. A type one error is when the fire alarm has gone off, but there is no fire. A false positive. Type 2 Error: This is a false negative. You read the CXR as negative because you thought the 7 cm mass was just a “funny-looking rib shadow.” Or using our fire alarm example you could think about this as - the house is on fire, but the alarm does NOT go off. Obviously this is the worst kind of error. Ways to Remember this: Type 2 Error will cost you “twice” as much when you get sued - remember that it’s the bad one. The missed cancer, or the missed fire. Type 2 Error is a false negative or a “false-false,” so “two” f…

S ta tis tica l Epidem iology Incidence: The number of NEW cases occurring in a particular time period. Number of NEW cases of disease Incidence Rate = ------------------------------------ Per unit time Total Number of People at Risk Prevalence: The number of cases of a certain disease in a particular moment in time. Number of cases of disease _ . _ . At a particular time Prevalence Rate = ------------------------------------ Total Number of People at Risk Sensitivity & Specificity Both sensitivity and specificity measure validity (the ability to detect people with or without disease). • True Positive (TP) = Test shows cancer, Patient has cancer • False Positive (FP) = Test shows cancer, Patient does NOT have cancer. * Same as a Type I error • True Negative (TN) = Test shows NO cancer, Patient does NOT have cancer. • False Negative (FN) = Test shows NO cancer, Patient has cancer. * Same …

Disease + Test I want to discourage you from using the A, B, C, D method for doing statistical problems. This can get you in hot water if the question writer flips the axis of the square (which they love to do). It’s just better to understand what you are actually measuring. Sensitivity = This is the ability o f the test to detect true disease. In other words, the amount of true disease detected with the test / total people with the disease. True Positive / True Positive + False Negatives. Specificity = This is the ability o f the test to detect people free o f disease. In other words, the amount of disease free people called negative by the test / total number of disease free people. True Negative / True Negative + False Positives A very specific test rules in disease. (SPIN ; SPecificity rules IN) 417

Accuracy = Think about this as how often the test was right: True Positive + True Negative / TP + FP + TN + FN Positive Predictive Value: This is the likelihood that a person with a positive test actually has the disease. True Positive / True Positive + False Positives Negative Predictive Value: This is the likelihood that a person with a negative test actually is disease free. True Negative / True Negative + False Negative PPV and NPV are influenced by the prevalence of disease. If we test in a high prevalence setting, it is more likely that a persons with a positive test result will truly have the disease, compared to the situation where we would test a population with low prevalence. In other words, if you see a spiculated nodule on the Chest CT of a 70 year old smoker it’s probably a cancer (true positive more likely in a patient population with high prevalence). Risk: Absolute Risk: The more common a disease is, the higher the risk of catching it. Most people will say that the incidence of a disease is the most important risk factor. That is why epidemiologists defined “absolute risk” as essentially the same thing as disease incidence. The incidence o f disease is 1:100, then the absolute risk is 1:100. Relative Risk: Ever wondered how many times the guy with multi-drug resistant TB can cough on you on your plane ride to Chicago (or Tucson) before your PPD converts? This is the best way to think about relative risk - exposure to risk factors increases the risk of getting the disease. RR = Incidence of disease among persons exposed to risk factor Incidence of disease among people who did NOT get exposed to risk factor. Absolute Risk Reduction: The general way to think about this is the difference between the people who did not get the drug (contr…

Odds Ratio: The absolute risk calculations require a prospective study. Odds ratio can be done using a retrospective study. The basic idea is “what are the odds that a disease will develop in an exposed person?” This is how to work it out: Number of Cases WITH Exposure Odds of exposure in case = Number of Cases WITHOUT Exposure Number of Controls WITH Exposure Odds of exposure in controls = Number of Controls WITHOUT Exposure Odds of Exposures in CASES Odds Ratio = ____________________________ Odds of Exposures in CONTROLS Cases WITH Exposure x Controls WITHOUT Exposure Odds Ratio= F Controls WITH Exposure x Cases WITHOUT Exposure 420

You want your level at the attenuation of the thing you are interested in. For example, if you are interested in bone - you want a high level. Widows Width Thing you change for Contrast This is selected based on what you are comparing. If you are comparing things with very different densities you want a wide width. If you are comparing things with very similar densities (example white and gray matter), you want a very narrow window width. Above the upper limit of the width everything will look white. Dose U nits Exposure (C/kg) Charge (C) in the air created divided by the mass (kg) of that air. Absorbed Dose (Gy) 1 Gy = 100 rads Energy deposited per kilogram of material (J/kg = Gy), Organ Dose = Total energy to that organ / Total mass of the organ. Equivalent Dose (Sv) Absorbed dose of different types of radiation creates different levels of biologic damage (thus measured in Sv). A weighting factor is used to adjust the value. For example, an alpha particle can do more damage than an electron. EqD = Dose x Weight Factor Weight Factor = for x-rays and gamma rays this is 1, Weight Factor = for alpha particles it’s 20. Effective Dose (Sv) Closest measure of cancer risk, since it considers both radiation and tissue type. This takes into account whether radiation has been absorbed by the specific tissue. In other words, you are taking into account the type of radiation and the variable se…

Deterministic Effects Stochastic Effects (“Random”) Has a threshold Has NO threshold Severity is dose related Severity is NOT dose related Probability of effect increases with dose Does Not include Cancer Risk Includes heritable effects and carcinogenesis (NOT cell killing) Dose U nits - Fluoro Air Kerma (AK) (Gy/min) Measures the intensity of the x-ray beam. Absorbed dose in Air Energy transfer from the primary interaction of photons on tissue atoms. NOT the secondary production of scatter electrons which also contribute to the overall dose. Relates to Deterministic Risk Decreases with distance from the x-ray source — inverse square. Kerma-Area Product (KAP) (Gy-cm2) Accounts for both exposure and area exposed - best thought of as “the whole beam.” KAP = Dose “AK” x Cross Sectional Area Relates to Stochastic Risk Independent of the source distance. You can measure it at any point along …

Dose U nits - CT CTDI “CT Dose Index” mGy This is the radiation dose, normalized to beam width. CTDI numbers are based on phantoms. The body phantom is 32 cm in diameter. If the patient is larger (a big fat pig) than the phantom, then dose is over estimated. If the patient is smaller (peds) than the phantom, then dose is under estimated. “Weighted CTDI” mGy This is 1/3 the central CTDI + 2/3 the Peripheral CTDI (expressed in mGy) “Volume CTDI” mGy This is obtained by dividing weighted CTDI by the Pitch. 2/3 CDTIp + 1/3 CTDIc / pitch “Reference Dose ” set by the ACR at 75 percentile CTDIvoi: • 75mGy for Head, • 25 mGy for Adult Abd, • 20 mGy for Peds Abd (5 year old) DLP “Dose Length Product” mGy - cm CTDI - Vol x the length of the scan in cm. It is appropriate to add DLP from series to series. “Effective Dose”fo r CT mSv Effective Dose = k x DLP. Remember that “k” is a body part constant…

MR - Safety Related Trivia Zone I No Restriction This is basically outside the building. Zone II No Restriction This is the waiting room and the dressing room. This is where you can screen patients and control access to Zone 3 and 4. Zone III Restricted Room This is typically the control room, where the MRI tech does his/her thing. There should be some kind of a lock on the door between zone 2 and 3. Zone IV Restricted Room This is the actual MRI scanner room (the same room as the magnet) Quenching - Only if ? There is a fire or trapped patients or staff member trapped Prior to the quench ... Get the fuck out of the room (zone 4) Quench if code ? Biostatistics - Trivia Summary Precision Accuracy Definition This is the immunity to variation This is the immunity to systematic error or bias Type 1 Error The is a false positive. The fire alarm has gone off, but there is no fire. Type 2 Error: Incidence Rate This is a false negative. The house is on fire, but the alarm does NOT go off. New Cases of Disease Total Number of People are Risk Prevalence Rate Sensitivity Number of Cases of Disease Total Number of People at Risk True Positive True Positive + False Negatives. *Sensitive Exams Rule Out Disease Specificity Accuracy True Negative True Negative + False Positives *Specific Exams Rule IN Disease True Positive + True Negative TP + FP + TN + FN Positive Predictive Value True Positive True Positive + False Positives Negative Predictive Value True Negative True Negative + False Negative 468
